channel.title: NoWhere Differentiable Math
channel.description: A blog about Mathematics, Spatial statistics, stochastic analysis, and PDEs.

item.title: PDEs 7: Parabolic PDE, well-posedness and regularity
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares-Elias
item.description:
1 Three point summary

  • A parabolic PDE describes how a function evolves over time under the influence of an elliptic operator. Unlike their elliptic counterparts, parabolic PDEs are typically well-posed. For well-behaved coefficients, a unique solution always exists and depends continuously on the initial data.

  • Solutions to parabolic PDEs can be viewed as functions mapping an instant in time to a function in a Banach space. From this viewpoint, the PDE becomes an infinite dimensional ODE. The Galerkin method is a powerful tool to prove the well-posedness of the problem and approximate its solutions using a finite-dimensional ODE that approximates the original infinite-dimensional problem.

  • The solution is smoother than the initial data and will always be at least continuous in time and (weakly) differentiable in space. If the coefficients of the PDE are smooth, the solution will be smooth as well.

2 Notation

  • As in the rest of the series, we will let denote an arbitrary open subset of . That is, it may be bounded or unbounded with no conditions on the regularity of the boundary (if it exists). To denote a smooth domain, we will use the notation .

  • We will be working with functions of time and space defined on some time interval and spatial domain . We will denote by the function .

  • Given a topological vector space with dual and we write the duality pairing

3 Introduction

In the elliptic case, we studied the well-posedness and regularity of solutions to an elliptic PDE of the form

where the second order differential operator was given in divergence form as

and verified the ellipticity condition. After defining the weak formulation of the problem, we naturally obtained the function spaces we were interested in and, under some restrictions on the coefficients, proved the existence and uniqueness of solutions. When solutions were not guaranteed to be unique, we studied the spectrum of , and in all cases, we showed that the solution had improved regularity as compared to the coefficients of the PDE.

In this post we now look to mimic the previous analysis but for parabolic PDEs. We define the operator

(by Leibnit’z rule one can move between (1) and (2)) and consider the parabolic PDE

where is the time interval of interest and . Here, we need an extra initial condition that tells us the initial state of the system. The operator has the same form as in (2), however now the coefficients are allowed to depend on both time and space. In the next sections, we will define the weak formulation of the problem and study the well-posedness and regularity of solutions. As we will see, within the functions spaces of interest, (3) is always well-posed. This is in contrast to elliptic PDEs, where well-posedness was not guaranteed in general.

4 Weak formulation

4.1 Banach valued functions

To define weak solutions to (3), it is convenient to switch our viewpoint. Instead of thinking of as a real-valued function of time and space, we think of it as a Banach space valued function of time

where is some Banach space of function on (such as ) and we use the notation . This way of viewing is an essential tool in the theory of evolution PDEs, which transform the PDE (3) into an infinite dimensional linear ODE.

To be able to proceed, we need to define an integral on functions valued in Banach spaces. This integral is called the Bochner integral. To briefly summarize, given a separable Banach space and we define

where functions equal almost everywhere are identified, and the norm is defined as

Then, hte spaces are Banach spaces and functions in have a well defined Bochner integral

This integral generalizes the Lebesgue integral and is also constructed by approximating the integrable functions by simple functions of the form where are sets with finite measure and .

Many familiar properties carry over to the Bochner integral. Namely, integration is a continuous linear operator on . Furthermore, given and , where is the conjugate exponent of , we can define the duality pairing

With this identification, the dual of is equal to . We will also use that if is a smooth approximation of unity then, for

To make the notation more readable we will use the convention of denoting function spaces in temporal and spatial domains by the subindexes . For example, we write

Due to the previous discussion we have that

A tricky aspect of PDE is figuring out what space should be? The choice of needs to be guided by what bounds one can obtain in the norm of . As we will see when we derive energy bounds for , the space is the natural Banach space to consider in this setting.

4.2 Weak solutions

As may have become familiar at this point of the series, to derive the weak formulation of our problem (3), we suppose that is smooth, multiply the equation by a test function and integrate over . We obtain that, in addition to the condition

where for brevity in the notation we omitted the customary in the integrals and wrote . Equivalently, with the notation for the duality pairing, we can write (7) and the boundary condition as

For the following theory we need some assumption on the coefficients and the data. Firstly, we give the following definition

Definition 1 We say that is parabolic if there exists a constant such that

This is the parabolic analog of the ellipticity condition we required in the previous post. Physically speaking, it guarantees that diffusion does not go to zero and occurs from regions of larger concentration to lower concentration.

Theorem 1 We assume that is parabolic and for all

The boundedness of is expedient so that the integrals in (8) are well defined.

For (8) to make sense and verify the boundary condition on , we give the following definition.

Definition 2 (Weak solution) Under Assumption Theorem 1, we say that is a weak solution of the parabolic problem (3) if

and (8) is verified for all .

With the above definition, all the terms (8) are well defined, where the duality pairing is to be interpreted as the one given by (6). Furthermore, the boundary condition on is automatically satisfied as for almost all and a quick sanity check shows that the various functional spaces in definition Definition 2 are logical as if then and from (3) we should also have . It only remains to justify that is well-defined. This is proved in the following lemma.

Lemma 1 Let with . Then, with

Proof. Let be a smooth approximation to the identity and define (we use the convention of extending by zero outside of so the convolution is well defined). Then, converges almost everywhere and in to and converges in to (see (5)).Given write . We have that,

As a result, by the fundamental theorem of calculus, given any ,

Taking any such that and the max over gives

where in the last equality we used that converge to in the respective norms.

Equation (10) shows that is a Cauchy sequence in the Banach space and, as a result, converges to a continuous function in . Since it also converges almost everywhere to , we must have that . This shows that .

To prove the bound of the theorem, suppose first that is smooth in . Then, by the fundamental theorem of calculus and the definition of the dual norm

The bound follows Cauchy-Schwartz, and the non-smooth case follows by approximating by smooth .

5 Well-posedness of the problem

We now aim to show that the problem (3), or more precisely its weak formulation (8), is well-posed.

5.1 A naive approach

As we have discussed in the previous section, equation (3) can be seen as an infinite dimensional linear ODE. As a result, we could hope that the theory of linear ODE will give us a solution. Working directly we would write and the equation (3) as

Then, writing once more , we could try to emulate Picard’s theorem for scalar-valued ODEs to obtain a fixed point for

The only problem with this is that does not map to . As a result, the mapping is not well defined. If the initial data is smooth, we could hope to set , and then would map to . However, is not a Banach space, so further modifications would be required. As a result, we need a more refined approach. In the next section, we use the Galerkin method to prove the problem’s well-posedness.

5.2 Galerkin solutions

Instead of working directly in infinite dimensions, we project our problem onto a finite-dimensional space spanned by basis functions . If we are able to solve the projected problem, we hope that as the number of basis functions increases, the solution will converge to the true solution. This is the idea behind the Galerkin method, which is widely used in the numerical study of PDEs.

Exercise 1 Use that is separable to show that it has a smooth orthonormal basis of functions . that is,

TipHint

Since is separable, it has a countable dense subset . Since is dense in , for each there exists a sequence that converges to in . Then, the set is a countable dense subset of , and we can apply the Gram-Schmidt process to obtain an orthonormal basis.

Alternatively, if is bounded and smooth, is compact and self-adjoint. Hence, it has a countable orthonormal basis of eigenfunctions which are smooth by the previous post and induction.

Let be an orthonormal basis of , let and let

Consider the problem of finding such that, for all and

where denotes the inner product in , is the pairing of an element in with an element in its dual and is the bilinear form on defined by

Equation (12) is known as the Galerkin problem.

Theorem 2 (Well-posedness of the Galerkin problem) Under Assumption Theorem 1, the Galerkin problem (12) is well-posed. That is, a unique solution exists and depends continuously on the initial data. Furthermore,

Proof. We divide the proof into three parts. Existence, continuity, and uniqueness.

  1. Existence of solutions: Since the are orthonormal and we impose , solving (12) is equivalent to finding such that

    where we define the matrix and vectors as

    The equivalence of solving (12) and (14) is obtained by setting .

    Since , by the boundedness of the coefficients in Assumption Theorem 1, and by the construction of . It holds that and . As a result, according to standard ODE theory, there exists a unique continuous solution to (14). One can even write out the explicit expression for using Duhamel’s formula,

    This proves existence.

  2. Continuity in the data: The plan will be to apply Gronwall’s inequality. By (12) and the linearity of the inner product, we obtain

    Now, since is smooth and by differentiating under the integral sign,

    Using Cauchy’s inequality , the boundedness of the coefficients and the ellipticity of shows that, for some constants

    (this is the same as what was proved as in the elliptic case). Whereas, by definition of the dual and by Cauchy’s inequality, the bound of the right-hand side of (16) is,

    Using (17), (18) and (19) in equation (16) we obtain that

    In particular,

    Given differentiable aa constants and integrable , Gronwall’s inequality states that

    Applying this to (21) gives

    Taking the maximum in (22) gives the first part of the bound in (13)

    To bound the second term in (13) we combine (22) with (20) to obtain

    Integrating over in (24) and applying the fundamental theorem of calculus together with (23) gives

    To bound consider any , and write where is the projection of onto with the inner product on . Since is orthogonal to for each , from (12) we have

    where we used that . We deduce that

    Integrating the square over and using (25) in (26) gives

    Now, combining (23) and (25), (27) and taking square roots, we obtain the desired bound (13).

  3. Uniqueness: To conclude uniqueness, let be two solutions to the Galerkin problem. Then, verifies the homogeneous problem

    The same reasoning that proved (21), where now , shows that

    Now, applying Grönwall’s inequality and by the initial condition , we obtain for some constant

    This shows that and hence . This proves uniqueness and concludes the proof.

Having proved the well-posedness of the Galerkin problem, we can now show that the parabolic problem is well-posed. A common technique in PDE is to modify your initial problem by some quantity to obtain a problem that is easier to solve. Suppose one can find solutions to that are bounded. In that case, a converging subsequence can typically be extracted and, under appropriate conditions, will converge to a solution to the original problem . This is exactly what we show now.

Theorem 3 (Well posedness of the parabolic problem) Under Assumption Theorem 1, the parabolic problem (3) is well-posed. That is, there exists a unique weak solution , which depends continuously on the initial data with

Proof. Let be the sequence of solutions to the Galerkin problem (12) guaranteed by Theorem (12). By said theorem, and are bounded sequences in and respectively. Since these spaces are Hilbert spaces, they are reflexive, and we deduce by the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banach

We first show that . We have that, given

where in the first equality we used the weak convergence of to in , in the second equality we used the definition of weak derivative, and in the last we used the convergence of to in . This shows that almost everywhere. We now show that solves the weak problem (8). By construction of the Galerkin solutions in (12) and integrating over , we deduce for any

As a result, taking limits in shows that, for all

Since the space is dense in we deduce that

We now check that . To do so, we now consider such that . Then, integrating by parts over and using (30) gives

Taking limits above and by density of in we obtain that

where we used that by Lemma 1 . In particular,

Since is dense in this shows that and concludes the proof.

Remark 1. It may seem like the conclusion is impossible. After all, we did not impose that . So how can we hope that for to be on if ? The solution lies in the fact that , but it may not hold that . As a result, we only know that for almost every . And it is not required that for .

6 Numerical illustrations

In this section, we show some numerical illustrations of the well-posedness of the parabolic problem. The code to calculate the numerical solutions and generate the figures using Mathematica can be downloaded by clicking here. We first consider the problem

where we take and . We use the Galerkin method and set as basis functions the normalized eigenfunctions of the Laplacian with zero boundary conditions on ,

We show solutions for and ; as we can see, as the number of basis functions increases, the solution converges to when and becomes quite oscillatory at the boundary to try to adapt to the admittedly somewhat incompatible boundary conditions.

We also include a figure to show how the boundary condition may be approximated in using the basis functions . We note that the approximating sequence does not converge as the derivative explodes at the boundary.

Next, we show a case where the exact solution can be calculated. We take and and consider the problem

The exact solution to (32) is . We show the exact solution and the Galerkin solution using

as before for basis functions. As we can see, the Galerkin solution and the exact solution are quite close.

7 Regularity of the solutions

Having proved the well-posedness of the parabolic problem (3) and its finite-dimensional Galerkin approximation, we can now move on to study the regularity of the solutions. The proof is similar to the one given in the elliptic case but more technical, and we will mainly cite some main results without proof.

As one expects from the elliptic case, the spatial regularity of the solutions is increased by . Our study of the previous section shows that the regularity of the time derivatives of the solution is orders lower. In fact, it is two orders lower for each time derivative taken. To be able to obtain higher regularity however, we will need to impose some compatibility between the boundary condition and the initial data . The following can be found in Section 7.13 of (Evans 2022)

Theorem 4 Let be a bounded domain with smooth boundary . Let be the elliptic operator in (2) and assume that the coefficients are independent of time and smooth in space. Suppose that

and the compatibility conditions

are satisfied. Then,

and we have the estimate

In consequence, if then .

We also show a result where the coefficients are allowed to depend on time. The following can be found in Chapter of (Friedman 1983) and assumes that the coefficients are Hölder continuous

Theorem 5 Let be a bounded domain with smooth boundary and assume that for all , . If is a classical solution of , then for all . In consequence, if then .

8 Conclusions

This ends our study of parabolic partial differential equations. With it, we bring this series on linear PDEs to a, at least momentary, end. We began our study with the Fourier transform, then ventured into the thick jungle of distributions, emerging into the open plains of Sobolev spaces. There, we lingered, marveling at its vast expanse and many corners, fractional and otherwise. Armed with newfound knowledge, we marched bravely into the land of elliptic PDEs and, with our trusty aide Lax-Milgram and a few carefully derived energy estimates, made short work of the enemy. In this final stop, we added time into the mix and, wielding the ideas of Bochner, Galerkin, and Grönwall (plus our ever-reliable bag of tricks), secured the well-posedness and regularity of solutions.

At times, our journey may have seemed arduous, long, and winding, but I hope you found it as rewarding as I did. Though we now move on to fresh pastures, our hard-earned map of the land of linear PDEs will surely serve us well in future adventures. Next, we turn our sights to new horizons, and there are many to choose from, such as the nature of probability, Bayesian inference, and stochastic partial differential equations. The road ahead is filled with possibilities, and I hope you’ll join us for this next leg of our journey.

9 References

Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.
Friedman, Avner. 1983. “Partial Differential Equations of Parabolic Type.” In. https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:117905500.

item.title: PDEs 6: Elliptic PDE
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three point summary

  • Elliptic partial differential equations (PDE) are PDE with no time variable and whose leading order derivatives satisfy a positivity condition.

  • Using Lax Milgram’s theorem, we can prove the existence and uniqueness of weak (distributional) solutions if the reaction term dominates the transport term. Using the Fredholm alternative, we can characterize the spectrum of the elliptic operator and the existence of solutions.

  • Under suitable smoothness assumptions on the coefficients and domain, the solution map of the PDE adds two derivatives to the input function. This improved regularity allows us to recover classical solutions if the coefficients are smooth enough.

2 Why should I care?

Many problems arising in physics, such as the Laplace and Poisson equation, are elliptic PDE. Furthermore, the tools used to analyze them can be extrapolated to other settings, such as parabolic PDE (depending on your viewpoint this may be a bit circular). The analysis also helps contextualize and provide motivation for theoretical tools such as Hilbert spaces, compact operators and Fredholm operators.

3 Notation

We will use Vinogradov notation to mean that there exists a constant such that . If we want to emphasize that the constant depends on a parameter , we will write .

We fix to be an open subset of with no conditions on the regularity of . If we need to impose regularity on the boundary, we will write instead of . Finally, we will write

4 Introduction

Welcome back to the second post on our series of PDE. In post 4, we gave a physical derivation of PDE (both parabolic and elliptic) that justify why we are interested in such equations. In posts 1, 2, 3, 5 of the series we built up the theoretical framework necessary to define Sobolev spaces, spaces of weakly differentiable functions to which we could extend the concept of differentiation (I know, the order is a bit messed up). We are now going to use the previous theory to study these equations.

5 The problem: Mathematical framework

We consider the following problem: given a bounded open set and some coefficients , we want to solve the following elliptic PDE

where is some known function, is the solution we want to find. In the case where the boundary condition is vacuous as .

We recall from post 4 that physically; we can interpret as the density of some substance, as a diffusion matrix, as a transport vector, as a reaction coefficient and as the source term. For the mathematical theory, we will need some conditions on the coefficients. Primarily, we require that is elliptic.

Definition 1 (Ellipticity) Given and we say that the operator

is elliptic if there exists such that

We also say that is elliptic.

There are some points to clear up. Firstly, if this is the first time you’ve encountered the ellipticity condition in (3), then it may seem a bit strange. With the previous physical interpretation, the ellipticity condition (3) says that diffusion occurs from the region of higher to lower density. Mathematically speaking, (3) will prove necessary to apply Lax Milgram’s theorem and obtain regularity estimates on .

Next, when developing the mathematical theory of any equation, the first step is to establish whether the equation is well-posed.

Definition 2 (Well-posedness) We say that an equation is well-posed if

  1. It has a solution.

  2. The solution is unique.

  3. The solution depends continuously on the data.

The above definition originates from the work of Hadamard and is standard in the context of PDE. The three properties above make the problem nice to work with and may be familiar from the basic theory of ODE. However, not all problems are well-posed. Ill-posedness often arises when one works with inverse problems, such as the backward heat equation, where one tries to recover the initial heat distribution from the final one.

The well-posedness of any given PDE is highly contingent on the space considered. In our case, we still need to define which function space our coefficients live in and what space acts on. It would be natural to assume that we need and to be differentiable. However, the following will suffice.

Theorem 1 We assume that for all . Furthermore, is symmetric () and elliptic.

In the future, will always run from to , where is the dimension of the space.

Remark 1. We lose no generality by assuming that is symmetric as . If is not symmetric, we can replace by and equation (1) will remain unchanged.

The first part of Assumption Theorem 1 will make it easy to get bounds on , and the second part will prove useful when we look at the spectral theory of . Now, to make sense of our problem (1), we need to define what we mean by a solution. Here, the theory of Sobolev Spaces and the Fourier transform prove crucial. We will work with the following space.

Definition 3 (Negative Sobolev space) Given we define

For more details on why we denote the dual using negative exponents, see the relevant section in the previous post on fractional Sobolev spaces. We recall also that every element in can be written as the sum of derivatives up to order of a function in .

Exercise 1 Suppose . Then, defines a bounded linear operator

TipHint

By definition of the weak derivative, show that given ,

Use this to conclude that,

So, is well defined and is bounded. Extend by density to .

Exercise 1 allows us to define the weak formulation of (1) and study its well-posedness using Lax Milgram’s theorem. We will do this in the next section.

6 Weak solutions and well-posedness

By Exercise 1, we can make sense of the equation for all .

Definition 4 (Weak formulation) Given , we say that solves equation (1) if

In (4) we used the “duality notation” for (here ). We have now reformulated our problem to something that looks very similar to the setup of Lax Milgram’s theorem and can use this to prove the well-posedness of (1) under certain conditions.

Theorem 2 Let be an arbitrary open set. Suppose Assumption Theorem 1 holds and let . Then, if equation (1) is well-posed, and we have the homeomorphism

If is bounded, the above also holds for .

Proof. The continuity of is a consequence of Exercise 1. It remains to see that is coercive. For smooth we have that

Where in the first inequality, we used the ellipticity assumption on , and in the last inequality, we used Poincaré’s inequality if is bounded. The result now follows from Lax Milgram’s theorem.

Theorem 2 is an example of the advantages of working with a weak formulation instead of solutions differentiable in a classical sense. The weak formulation allows us not only to make sense of our equation (1) for a wider class of coefficients but also provides a natural framework to study the well-posedness of (1).

Exercise 2 Show that, under the conditions of Theorem 2, if is bounded, there is a countable basis of eigenfunctions for .

TipHint

By Rellich’s theorem is compact and, since is , is also is self adjoint. As a result, so there is a countable basis of eigenvectors in .

In Theorem 2, we somewhat unsatisfyingly had to impose the extra assumption that was identically zero and that . These extra assumptions can be done away with but at the cost of modifying our initial problem by a correction term so we can obtain a coercive operator .

Theorem 3 (Modified problem) Let be any open set and let Assumption Theorem 1 hold. Then, there exists some constant (depending on the coefficients) such that for all the operator is positive definite and defines a homeomorphism

That is, the problem is well-posed for all .

Proof. Once more, the proof will go through the Lax-Milgram theorem, where now we work with the bilinear operator associated with

The calculation proceeds similarly to (5). We recall the Cauchy inequality

which can be checked directly by using that . Applying (6) to and , shows that

Taking small enough (smaller than to be precise) and gathering up terms gives

Where we defined . The theorem follows from (7) as for all

Equation (8) also shows that is positive definite and the proof is complete.

6.1 Fredholm alternative

We now analyze further what we can say about the well-posedness of (1). What has to happen for the equation to be ill-posed? If there are multiple solutions, what does the space of solutions look like? As we will see, this is intimately linked to the spectrum of the operator and the Fredholm alternative will provide the answers we are looking for,

We begin by considering , which is a small generalization of our original problem (1). We take large enough as in Theorem 3 and note that

Now write and rename . An algebraic manipulation shows that (9) is equivalent to

where is the identity operator. Suppose now that is bounded, then, by Rellich’s theorem, we know that the following inclusion is compact

As a result, by Theorem 2, we deduce that , which we are now viewing as an operator on , is compact. More precisely,

is compact and the reasoning in (9), (10) shows that, given , and

Equation (11) is exactly the form the Fredholm alternative takes ( can be incorporated into the compact operator ) and justifies the following.

Theorem 4 Let be bounded, let verify Assumption Theorem 1, and be any. Consider the problems

Then, the following hold:

  1. Equation (12) is well-posed if and only if (12) has no non-zero solutions. That is, if and only if .

  2. The spectrum is discrete. If is infinite, then .

  3. The dimensions of the following spaces are equal

  4. Equation, (12) has a solution if and only if equivalently for all .

Proof. Given and as before, we consider large and define

, where is the inclusion. Consider the following two problems,

The reasoning in (11) showed that a solution to (12) gives a solution to (13) via the transformation . The converse needs to be clarified, as given , the inverse transformation may not return a function in . However, if solves (13), then verifies

Cancelling out the and dividing by we obtain that

By Theorem 2 we know that for all . As a result, solves problem (12), and by the transformation problem (13) has a solution if and only if problem (12) has a solution. Taking , we also obtain that solves problem (12) if and only solves problem (13). In conclusion,

Problem (12) is w.p Problem (13) is w.p ,

where the second equivalence is due to the Fredholm alternative, and the third can be verified by an algebraic manipulation. This proves the first point.

To see the second point, note that, by definition of , equation (13) has non-zero solutions if and only if . Since is compact, is discrete and if is infinite, then its eigenvalues, which we denote by , go to . Furthermore, since by Theorem 3 is positive definite, and the claim follows by the correspondence .

For the third and fourth points, we use that, as we have already proved, . Additionally,

from where

Applying the Fredholm alternative concludes the proof.

Setting in Theorem 4, we recover our original problem and obtain the following corollary.

Corollary 1 Equation (1) is well-posed unless the homogeneous problem has a non-zero solution (that is, ). The space of solutions then has dimension , which is also equal to the dimension of . Finally, (1) will have a solution if and only if is orthogonal to the kernel of .

In particular, to study the existence of solutions to (1), it is enough to study the uniqueness of solutions to (1)!

Exercise 3 In Theorem 4 we used that, for large enough, is compact. However, is invertible with inverse . As a result is compact. How is this possible?

TipHint

In fact, is only invertible as an operator from . However, it is not invertible as an operator from . Given , it is not generally possible to find an such that .

Exercise 4 Where does the proof of Theorem 4 break down if we replace with ?

TipHint

Can you apply Rellich’s theorem to unbounded domains? What is the spectrum of the Laplacian on ?

Exercise 5 Show using Theorem 4 that equation (13) (the generalization of (1)) is well-posed saved for at most a discrete set of .

TipHint

Combine the first and second points of Theorem 4.

Exercise 6 Show the necessity of point 4 in Theorem 4 using only linear algebra.

TipHint

Suppose and . Then,

7 Higher regularity

We have so far seen that, under the previous assumptions, solutions to (1) are in . However, analogously to the classical setting, we may expect that is two degrees of regularity smoother than . That is that . This improved regularity is true, but only with the caveat that the domain is sufficiently regular. Counterexamples with non-smooth domains exist. See (Savaré 1998).

We will also see how, for smoother coefficients, we can iterate to obtain a higher regularity of . As a result, when the coefficients of (1) are smooth, will be as well, and we will obtain a classical solution to problem (1).

7.1 Finite differences

In our study of regularity, we will make use of the difference quotients. Given a function , we define the difference quotients in the -th direction as

If is differentiable, then as . The following lemma shows that, on , the difference quotients of are bounded if and only if is weakly differentiable.

Lemma 1 (Difference quotients and regularity) Let , and be some constant. Then, the following hold.

  1. If and for all sufficiently small . Then .

  2. If . Then, .

Proof. We begin by proving the first point. Since is reflexive, every bounded sequence in has a weakly convergent subsequence. Thus, we can find and such that weakly in . We want to show that . To this aim, let . Then,

where in the first equality, we used the weak convergence of to ; in the second, we separated the integral in two and used the change of variable on the first of the integrals (from now on we will call this “discrete integration by parts”). The final equality follows from the smoothness of . Since was arbitrary, we have that almost everywhere. Since , this shows that .

For the second point, suppose that is smooth; then, by the fundamental theorem of calculus,

Taking norms and using <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minkowski_inequality#:~:text=.-,Minkowski

where in the second equality, we used the change of variables . We conclude by using the density of smooth functions in to take limits in the above inequality.

The result can be extended to arbitrary open subsets . In this case, one can only obtain local regularity as the translation is not well defined on the whole of . We recall the notation to mean that is a subset of with .

Lemma 2 (Difference quotients and local regularity) Let , be a constant and open. Then, the following hold.

  1. If and for all sufficiently small . Then, .

  2. If . Then, for all .

Exercise 7 Prove Lemma 2.

TipHint

Adapt the proof of Lemma 1. Take for the first point. For the second point, use the local density of smooth functions in .

7.2 Regularity on

By using second-order finite difference, we now show that the solution to (1) is in if we impose additionally that is continuously differentiable.

Theorem 5 (Improved regularity on ) Suppose that is elliptic and that . Then, if solves , it holds that with

Proof. The idea is to use difference quotients to approximate the second derivative of

Since , we can substitute into the weak formulation (4), do a discrete integration by parts and use Cauchy’s inequality to show that is bounded. Using Lemma 1, we will then conclude that and finish off the proof. We now put this plan into action. From (4), we have that

Applying a discrete integration by parts to the left-hand side of (15) as in (14), we obtain

where in the last equality, we used the notation and the product rule for difference quotients (this can be checked by basic algebra). Using the ellipticity of and Cauchy’s inequality (6) to put on the higher order negative term we obtain that for some constant

where we used that, since , the term is bounded. Setting in (16) we obtain that

We now estimate the right-hand side of (15). We have that, by Cauchy’s inequality and the second point of Lemma 1,

Once more, setting gives

Using (17) and (18) in (15) shows that, for some constant ,

Equation (19) is almost the desired result save the presence of on the right-hand side. However, by setting in (15) and once more using Cauchy’s inequality, we obtain that

Combining (19) and (20) gives the bound

Applying the first point of Lemma 1 and taking square roots concludes the proof.

Iterating the above theorem, we obtain that if the coefficients of are smooth, then the solution to (1) is smooth as well. And is a classical solution to (1).

Theorem 7 (Infinite regularity on ) Let with elliptic. Then, if solves , it holds that

Exercise 8 The following argument is false. Show the flaw in the reasoning.

Let be any open subset. Suppose that is elliptic and that . Let solve . The extension to by zero of is in . The coefficients can likewise be extended by to functions . Likewise for to and by Assumption, is the restriction to of some function . We have that

Are you sure that solves (25)? Consider for example the case . For to solve (25) it is necessary that for all

That is, that

Whereas we only know that solves (4). That is,

This equality does not imply the previous one. The problem is that extension by zero does not respect the second derivative of functions in . For example, if we do not necessarily have that is in . Consider for example and . Then, solves our equation (1) with and, given ,

7.3 Interior regularity

We have just seen that a direct generalization of Theorem 5 to unbounded domains is not possible using an extension by zero. However, by adapting the proof of Theorem 5, one can prove the analogous result.

In this case, however, one has to be careful as the difference quotients may not be well defined at the boundary. As a result, it is necessary to work locally and use a bump function. This makes the proofs a bit messier, though the idea is the same. We sketch the proof, which can also be found in (Evans 2022) page 326

Theorem 8 (Improved interior regularity) Let be a solution to where , is elliptic and . Then, and

Note that we do not require to be in .

Proof. Let be open and let be a bump function supported on and identically equal to on . For small we have that

Proceeding as in the proof of Theorem 5, we obtain that

Applying the first point of Lemma 2 we obtain that with

Analogously, we also obtain by setting that

Combining (27) and (28), we obtain the desired result.

As we did in the case , we can obtain higher-order regularity by induction. As before, we now write

In the case that is bounded then .

Theorem 9 (Interior regularity) Suppose that is elliptic and that its coefficients verify

Then, if solves , it holds that with

Exercise 9 Prove Theorem 9.

TipHint

The theorem holds for by Theorem 8. Suppose by hypothesis of induction that the theorem holds up to order . Let

Then, by the induction hypothesis with

Let be open, consider a multi-index with and . Then, substituting in the weak formulation (4) we obtain by integrating by parts that

Let us write . Applying the chain rule repeatedly and keeping only the derivatives of order of on the left-hand side to obtain

where involves only as well as sums and products of derivatives up to order of and up to order of and . As a result, by the conditions on the coefficients in (29) and the induction hypothesis (30), we have that with

By equation (31), is a solution to on and applying (30) and (32) shows that with

Using Sobolev embeddings we obtain once more infinite regularity for smooth coefficients.

Theorem 10 (Infinite interior regularity) Let with elliptic. Then, if solves , it holds that .

Proof. By Theorem 9, we have that for all . By Sobolev embeddings we have that .

7.4 Regularity at the boundary

Regularity at the boundary can also be obtained; however, in this case, it is necessary to impose the boundary condition . We can then work on bounded smooth domains by reasoning first on and then using a finite covering of and a change of coordinates to translate these results back to .

We summarize and sketch the proofs of the main results, which are analogous to the interior regularity results of Theorems Theorem 8, Theorem 9 and Theorem 10. The details For the partition to be finite, it is further necessary for to be bounded. We sketch the proof which can be found in (Evans 2022) pages and (Gilbarg et al. 1977) pages 183-188.

Theorem 11 (Improved regularity at the boundary) Let be bounded with . Let be elliptic and . Let be a weak solution to (1). Then, with

Proof. Since is of class , given there exists and a twice differentiable diffeomorphism

that maps the interior of to the interior of and the boundary to the boundary. That is,

Let us define on . Then, verifies a PDE of the same form as our original PDE (1). Where now the boundary condition holds only on the straight part straight part .

Since our boundary condition does not hold on the curved part of the boundary , to integrate by parts we need to introduce a bump function which is zero on .

Let and define . Now choose a bump function with compact support in (in particular, on ) and identically equal to on . Then, for small , we define as in (26) the function

Here, the increments are only well defined for . Proceeding as in the proof of Theorem 8, we obtain that, for ,

Since by Theorem 8, we have that the equality holds almost everywhere in . As a result,

where only involves derivatives up to order in of and up to order in of . Using the ellipticity of with in (3) we obtain that, almost everywhere in ,

Taking norms and using (33) gives

If we write , we have that with

Since is compact, we can cover with a finite number of such sets plus some open . We conclude the proof by using the interior regularity result of Theorem 8 to bound .

The following can now be proved by induction, just as in Theorems Theorem 8 and Theorem 9.

Theorem 12 (Higher regularity at the boundary) Let be bounded with . Let be elliptic and and . Let be a weak solution to (1). Then, with

Once more, by Sobolev embeddings we obtain infinite regularity at the boundary.

Theorem 13 (Infinite regularity at the boundary) Let be bounded of class . Let with elliptic. Let be a weak solution to (1). Then, .

8 Other boundary conditions

So far, we have only worked with homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions. However, it often makes more sense to work with the non-homogeneous case. We also show how to extend the results to Neumann or Robin boundary conditions. In this section, we will see how to adapt the results of the previous section to these cases.

8.1 Non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions

Consider the problem with non-homogeneous Dirichlet boundary conditions on a domain with with Lipschitz boundary, written

For (34) to hold, it is necessary that is the restriction to of some function called a lifting of onto (in particular it is the restriction of to ). That is, where is the trace operator and is the desired regularity of . By theory of the trace operator, we know that this is equivalent to . Then, by forming we obtain that solves the homogeneous Dirichlet problem

where

The transformation maintains regularity up to order for and as a result, we can apply all the previous results on well-posedness, the spectrum of and regularity to the problem (34). For example, the following holds.

Theorem 14 Let be a bounded domain with boundary then

  1. The non-homogeneous Dirichlet problem (34) is well-posed for if and only if the homogeneous problem (1) is well posed.

  2. The Fredholm alternative (Theorem 4) holds where we replace by the subspace of whose trace is equal to .

  3. If , then under the same conditions of Theorem 12 on and the coefficients of .

8.2 Neumann and Robin boundary conditions

Consider a bounded domain with boundary of type (Lipschitz boundary). The elliptic Robin boundary condition problem is

The Neumann boundary condition problem is the case where . An integration by parts shows that the weak formulation of (35) is

For the weak form to be well defined we need for to be in the dual space (this is different from )and . A similar reasoning to previously shows the following.

Theorem 15 Let be a bounded domain, and .

  1. Let and . Suppose both are not identically zero. Then the Robin boundary condition problem (36) has a unique solution .

  2. The well posedness of the modified problem and the Fredholm alternative of Theorem 3 and Theorem 4 hold swapping everywhere with and with .

  3. Let . If solves (35) and

    Then, with

Proof. Under the conditions of Point , the bilinear form is coercive (this can be show by a proof by contradiction see for example (Mikhailov 1978) page 146). As a result, the Lax-Milgram theorem implies the existence of a unique solution .

The second point is a repetition of what was already shown for the homogeneous Dirichlet problem, for large , the modified bilinear form si coercive and the Fredholm alternative holds.

The third point can be seen by first repeating the proof of Theorem 11 where with the notation of this theorem, now solves

As a result, integrating by parts against is valid for small and and gives

Using a discrete integration by parts, Cauchy’s inequality and the regularity of the coefficients, we obtain the bound

Using a covering of gives and the result follows by induction as in Theorem 12.

8.2.1 The Dirichlet problem with Neumann boundary conditions

As an example of some interest, in the simple case where is the identity and , are zero (the Poisson equation with Neumann boundary conditions), the weak formulation is

Note carefully that is not coercive on as Poincaré’s inequality does not hold for Neumann boundary conditions.

Exercise 10 Show that has the eigenvalue . Show that, if decomposes into connected components , then the eigenspace of is -dimensional and spanned by the indicator functions . In particular, the eigenspace of is one-dimensional if is connected.

TipHint

Since is bounded and . Let be an eigenfunction of with eigenvalue . Then, by the weak formulation of the Laplacian, we have that

Exercise 11 Let decompose into connected components . Show that, for , the Laplace equation has a weak solution if and only if

Furthermore, show that the solution is unique up to a constant on each connected component .

9 References

Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.
Gilbarg, David, Neil S Trudinger, David Gilbarg, and NS Trudinger. 1977. Elliptic Partial Differential Equations of Second Order. Vol. 224. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-642-61798-0.
Mikhailov, V. P. 1978. Partial Differential Equations. Mir Publishers. https://archive.org/details/MikhailovPartialDifferentialEquations.
Savaré, Giuseppe. 1998. “Regularity Results for Elliptic Equations in Lipschitz Domains.” Journal of Functional Analysis 152 (1): 176–201. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002212369793158X/pdf?md5=c646200fe7117dd7d25d27439f36b342&pid=1-s2.0-S002212369793158X-main.pdf.

item.title: PDEs 5: Fractional Sobolev spaces
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.link: https://nowheredifferentiable.com/posts/PDEs/5. Fractional and negative order Sobolev spaces/
item.description:
1 Summary

  1. There are three ways to define Sobolev spaces with fractional regularity and integrability : \begin{enumerate}

  2. The spaces are defined by using the analogous to the definition of H"older spaces. Both spaces are equal when is not an integer.

  3. The space is defined by using the Fourier transform and coincides with for integer .

  4. All these spaces coincide with when .

There is a natural correspondence between negative regularity and the dual. Additionally, negative regularity can be obtained by differentiating functions with higher regularity.

Fractional sobolev spaces appear naturally in the study of PDEs. For example, the trace of Sobolev functions is equal to the fractional space . And finer embeddings and regularity results can be obtained by using these spaces. \end{enumerate}

2 Introduction

In previous posts, we covered the theory of Sobolev spaces where is an integer. In the case and when we saw that this space coincided with . Furthermore, we also saw how to define when was any real number. This motivates the following two questions.

  1. How can we define when is not and is not an integer ?

  2. Is it possible to extend such a definition to other orders of integrability ?

In this post, we aim to answer these questions. We will see that both of these questions can be answered in the affirmative. If the domain is smooth enough, the first point can be resolved by restricting functions in to . The second point is trickier and, in fact, like any good trick question, has multiple answers. Three, to be precise. This leads to the theory of Bessel spaces, Sobolev-Slobodeckij spaces and Besov spaces

We will cover the basic properties of these spaces as well as their relationship to each other with a special focus on which is the most widely used. We will see how these spaces can be used to obtain finer regularity results, such as in the trace theorem or Sobolev embeddings. The material in this post is mostly based on (Leoni 2023), (Agranovich 2015), (Di Nezza, Palatucci, and Valdinoci 2012), (Triebel 1992). The material can be quite technical, and there are multiple plus page books on the subject, so in many cases, we will state the main results, providing references for the proofs, as well as proving some of the more tractable results.

2.1 Preliminaries

In terms of notation, we will always denote by an arbitrary open subset of whereas will be open with a smooth enough boundary (in a sense to be made precise later).

We make frequent use of the fact that, as shown in a previous post, functions in can be identified as elements of the larger space of distributions . This is done by identifying a function with the linear functional

This identification between and allows us to extend by duality operators that are defined on to . For example, given we can define its Fourier transform and -th derivative to be the distributions defined by

The above definition is justified by the fact that if it turns out that is smooth and integrable enough after all, this coincides with the usual definitions of .

3 Fractional Sobolev spaces: three definitions

The definitions developed in the next three subsections can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 222.

3.1 Sobolev-Slobodeckij spaces

Definition 1 (Sobolev-Slobodeckij spaces) Let where , and . Then, given and an arbitrary open we define

where

For , we define . The norm is then given by

Remark 1. In the case where , by a change of variables, we can equivalently define

If this is not possible as may leave the domain where is defined.

We will later define also for negative (see Definition Definition 8). We observe that the above definition coincides with our usual definition of Sobolev space when and mimics that of the H"older spaces, coinciding exactly when .

Exercise 1 Show that is a Banach space.

TipHint

To show that is a norm apply Minkowski’s inequality to and to

Given a Cauchy sequence show that, since is complete, in and that in to conclude that in .

Though the Sobolev-Slobodeckij spaces can be defined for any open set , they are most useful when or is regular enough. Otherwise, basic properties such as the following break down

Proposition 1 (Inclusion ordered by regularity) Let be an extension domain for . Then, for and it holds that

The proof can be found in (Di Nezza, Palatucci, and Valdinoci 2012) page 10. The regularity of the domain is necessary to be able to extend functions in to . The result is not true otherwise, and an example is given in this same reference.

3.2 Bessel potential spaces

We now give a second definition of fractional Sobolev spaces through the Fourier transform. Here, it is immediately possible to define everything for negative .

Definition 2 Let and . We define the Bessel potential operator by

In the definition above, we used the notation . As we saw when we studied Sobolev spaces through the Fourier transform, using the fact that is an isometry which transforms differentiation into polynomial multiplication

Equation (3) motivates the following extension to general .

Definition 3 (Bessel potential spaces on ) Let and , we define the Bessel potential space

and give it the norm

By construction, .

Exercise 2 Show that . Use this to show that the following is an invertible isomorphism

TipHint

Use that and show that the inverse of is .

We now extend this to open domains

Definition 4 (Bessel potential spaces on ) Let be open. We define

and give it the norm

The restriction above is in the sense of distributions. That is, we define the restriction of to as the distribution such that

Remark 2. It is tempting to define . However, since the Fourier transform, and thus , is a nonlocal operator, the norm would depend on the extension of to and be ill-defined.

Remark 3. It would also make sense to define through complex interpolation. This is likely different from the above definition, however, as we will later see, this will coincide with the definition above when is smooth enough (for example Lipschitz). See also (Leoni 2023) page 328 for a similar remark.

3.3 Besov spaces

Definition 5 (Besov spaces) Let where , and . Then, given and be an arbitrary open set we define

where

For , we define .

The above definition is extremely similar in form to that of the Sobolev-Slobodeckij spaces Definition 1. In fact, it is equivalent when . The difference is that in the definition of Besov spaces Definition 5, we require that . As a result, always . We have chosen to indicate this fact by the index “” on . An equivalent definition is possible which extends the above to negative values of

Definition 6 (Besov spaces, negative ) Let and choose any with . Then, given we define

The requirement is necessary as .

Exercise 3 Show that, for all the operator defines an invertible isomorphism

TipHint

Use definition Definition 6 and that has inverse .

The definition of can then be extended to general open sets and in the same way as for the Bessel potential spaces, once more the same observations apply.

Definition 7 (Besov spaces on ) Let be an smooth. We define,

and give it the norm

Remark 4. Different authors use different notations for these spaces. For example, in (Triebel 1992), the notation is used. With this notation, one has that, for , and ,

This clashes with the definition of integer-valued Sobolev spaces, so we do not use this notation. Other notations which can be found are the notation and . See (Stein 1970) and (Biccari, Warma, and Zuazua 2018).

3.4 Extension domains

Though it is possible to define fractional spaces for any open set, these are most useful when the domain is regular enough. We begin by characterizing the set of extension domains for . The following result can be found in (Leoni 2017) page 313.

Theorem 1 (Plump sets are extension domains) Let be an open connected set, and consider , and . Then, is an extension domain for if and only if there exists a constant such that for all and all . Where is the Lebesgue measure on .

For higher orders of regularity, the following is sufficient: see (Leoni 2023) and (Sawano et al. 2018) section 5.1.

Theorem 2 Let be open with uniformly Lipschitz boundary and consider . Then, is an extension domain for .

3.5 Interpolation

Both the Sobolev-Slobodeckij and Bessel potential spaces can be viewed as a way to fill the gaps between integer-valued Sobolev spaces. The following uses the concept of complex interpolation. We will not go into detail as the results will not be essential to us, but merely serve as a nice way to understand the relationship between the spaces.

Proposition 2 (Interpolation) Let , and

Then, given an extension domain it holds that

where denotes the complex interpolation space.

The result can be found in (Triebel 1992) page 45 for . The general result follows by extension. In particular, if we write and , then

4 Relationship between the definitions

The following result shows the inclusions between and can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 224 and in (Stein 1970) page 155.

Theorem 3 Let and an extension domain for . Then,

where the above inclusions are continuous and dense. Furthermore,

In consequence, for ,

The equality in (4) shows that, as long as we understand the behaviour of and , we can completely determine that of . It also justifies the following extension of to negative regularity.

Definition 8 (Slobodeckij space negative ) Let be an extension domain for . Then, given and any we define

The equality for in (5) justifies that, for sufficiently regular domains, all three spaces are written . We will prove the left-hand side of this equivalence in Exercise 4. For , the inclusions are, in general, strict. An example is constructed in (Stein 1970) page 161 exercise 6.8.

Exercise 4 (Equivalence of fractional spaces) Show without using Theorem 3 that

The above suggests that, for , the integrals appearing in the definition of the Slobodeckij spaces Definition 1 correspond to differentiating a fractional amount of times. This indeed is the case

5 Dual of Sobolev spaces and correspondence with negative regularity

Negative orders of regularity correspond to the dual of Sobolev spaces. This is best seen in the integer case. We first introduce the notation for the closure of in respectively. We also introduce the notation for the conjugate exponent of . We then have the following result (see (Evans 2022) pages 326-344 for the case ).

Theorem 4 For all and and an extension domain for , it holds that

The first equality will be discussed in the next subsection and is most easily proved when , in which case one can use the homeomorphism together with the reflexivity of . The second equality is a direct consequence of the integer order equality of Theorem 3. For fractional order regularities, we have the following result, which can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 228.

Theorem 5 Given and an extension domain, it holds that the spaces are reflexive Banach spaces with duals

where given a space of distributions on we define as the space of distributions on which are supported in . In particular, for ,

Remark 5. Some authors define given and

See, for example, (Biccari, Warma, and Zuazua 2018), (Leoni 2023) page 228. The definition in (7) is equivalent to Definition Definition 8 when or when . However, in other cases, the two definitions are not equivalent.

5.1 The dual of $H^{s,p

(^d)$} For some motivation, we start by considering the case . Note that, in this setting, .

TipHint

However, the composition is , which is hardly the identity mapping.

For another example where confusion with this kind of identification can arise, see remark 3 on page 136 of (Brezis and Brézis 2011).

5.2 The dual of $H^{s,p

_0()$} Given an extension domain and , one can define extension and restriction operators,

which verify . As a result, the restriction is surjective, and we can factor as

where given a closed set we define

the support being understood in the sense of distributions Now, given a Banach space and a closed subspace , elements of can be restricted to , obtaining functionals in . The kernel of this restriction is . Since, by the Hahn Banach theorem, the restriction is surjective, we obtain the factorization

Applying this to we obtain the result of Theorem 4.

where the second equality is by Exercise 6. This coincides well with what we observed in (8) and justifies an extension of (8) for negative .

As a final note, if , then and are not equal. Rather,

See (Taylor 2013) Section 4 for more details.

6 Representation theorems

We know that we can homeomorphically map the spaces and to the lower order spaces and by application of (differentiating times). In other words, spaces of lower-order regularity are obtained by differentiating functions with higher regularity. We show how to extend this idea to smooth domains in some particular cases.

Theorem 6 (Representation of ) Let be an extension domain for where and . Then, every element in is the unique extension of a distribution of the form

Proof. Define the mapping

where is the number of multi-indices bounded by . Where the notation says that we send to the vector formed by all its derivatives. By our definition of the norm on , we have that is an isometry and, in particular, continuously invertible on its image. Denote the image of by . Given we define

By Hahn Banach’s theorem, we can extend from to a functional and by the Riesz representation theorem, we have that there exists a unique such that

By construction, it holds that, for all

In particular, this holds for and if we set we obtain that for all

(we recall the notation for the duality pairing). By definitions of the norm on and Cauchy Schwartz, we have that is continuous with respect to the norm on and so we may extend it uniquely to the closure of in which is . By (10), the extension is necessarily . This completes the proof.

The above theorem shows that can be equivalently formed by differentiating times functions in . The proof also sheds some light as to why is the dual of and not the dual of . The reason is that given a sufficiently regular distribution in , it has a unique continuous extension to an element of , but not to an element of . We note however that, though the extension from to is unique, the functions will not be, for example, if it is possible to add a constant to and still obtain the same result.

The above results extend to Besov spaces; see (Agranovich 2015) page 227. This gives,

7 Some applications: Trace, embeddings and regularity

7.1 Trace operator

Consider a PDE of the form

Then, it is necessary to know exactly what boundary data is admissible. Suppose that is of order so we require . Will (11) have a solution? To be able to answer this question, we need to know the image of the trace operator. If , then there is no hope of finding a solution. The following theorem characterizes the image of the trace operator and can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 228 and (Leoni 2023, 229:350, 492).

Theorem 8 (Fractional trace theorem) Let be open with boundary. Then, for all , the trace operator can be extended from to a bounded operator

Furthermore, given , there exists such that with

Note that, since we have equality of with and for integer and non-integer respectively, we can also extend

7.2 Fractional Sobolev embeddings

In this section, we state the fractional analogue of the Sobolev embedding theorems for regularity . Here, the analogous of the exponent is

Definition 10 Given and , with , we define the Sobolev critical exponent by

The natural extension of the Sobolev embedding theorem to the fractional case is the following. First, we introduce the following notation for the fractional seminorm.

See (Leoni 2023) page 262 for the following result.

Theorem 9 (Fractional Sobolev-Gagliardo-Niremberg) Given an extension domain for and , it holds that

In particular, by interpolation, for all .

The critical case corresponding to is now (see (Leoni 2023) page 265)

Theorem 10 Given an extension domain for and , it holds that

We are in the subcritical case for . Extend to to form . Then, by Proposition 1, we have

The supercritical case can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 224 and (Leoni 2023) page 275. To simplify the notation we use the convention that stands for .

Theorem 11 (Morrey’s fractional embedding) Let be an extension domain for , we have a continuous embedding

This embedding also holds for provided that is non-integer.

As in the non-fractional case, one can also consider higher smoothness on the right-hand side (see (Leoni 2023) page 290).

Theorem 12 (Sobolev embedding into higher smoothness) Let be an extension domain for . Then, given and , it holds that

Finally, interpolation results are also possible. See (Leoni 2023) page 300.

Theorem 13 Let be an extension domain for and consider and . Then

for all , where and

The above results can also be formulated in terms of the Sobolev seminorm. For example, Theorem 13 can be formulated as

Higher order embeddings and interpolations can be obtained from the previous cases with regularity parameter below 1 in combination with the integer case.

Exercise 11 (Fractional Sobolev Embeddings) Let be an extension domain for . Given and , set and . The following continuous embeddings hold:

where , , and if or is any otherwise.

TipHint

Observe the algebraic relation:

The result follows from applying Theorem 9 and Theorem 11 sequentially with the integer-case embeddings. For the first (the subcritical) case, the fractional embedding Theorem 9 yields:

Applying the integer Sobolev embedding to , we conclude:

For the second (critical) case , the same bootstrapping yields . Since , the integer critical embedding applies.

For the supercritical case (), apply the fractional Morrey embedding directly, or bootstrap to an integer space where , and apply the integer Morrey embedding to extract the precise Hölder regularity .

Below we plot the Sobolev critical exponent for and . As we can see, it decreases with and increases with . This means that the larger the dimension the more regularity we need to obtain a bound on the same norm. The integrability increasing to infinity around the critical threshold .

Exercise 12 Let be an extension domain for . Show that, given and , it holds that

TipHint

Deduce that

For more higher order embeddings see also (Leoni 2023) Section 11.4. Finally, embeddings can be similarly formulated for Besov spaces. See (Sawano et al. 2018) page 219 for the following result.

Theorem 14 (Embedding for Besov spaces) Let be an extension domain, and consider . Then,

This and other results can be formulated for the more general spaces . Where . See, (Triebel 1992), (Agranovich 2015), (Sawano et al. 2018).

We conclude this post by commenting that given a second-order PDE with smooth coefficients, such as

One expects that is two degrees more regular than . That is, if , then we should have . This is indeed the case locally. However, to obtain smoothness up to the boundary, one also needs to be regular enough. In this case, Lipschitz continuity of is not sufficient, even if (see for example (Savaré 1998)). We may comment on this later in a future post.

8 References

Agranovich, Mikhail S. 2015. Sobolev Spaces, Their Generalizations and Elliptic Problems in Smooth and Lipschitz Domains. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-319-14648-5.
Biccari, Umberto, Mahamadi Warma, and Enrique Zuazua. 2018. “Local Regularity for Fractional Heat Equations.” Recent Advances in PDEs: Analysis, Numerics and Control: In Honor of Prof. Fernández-Cara’s 60th Birthday, 233–49. https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-319-97613-6_12.
Brezis, Haim, and Haim Brézis. 2011. Functional Analysis, Sobolev Spaces and Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 2. 3. Springer.
Di Nezza, Eleonora, Giampiero Palatucci, and Enrico Valdinoci. 2012. “Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Fractional Sobolev Spaces.” Bulletin Des Sciences Mathematiques 136 (5): 521–73. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007449711001254.
Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.
Leoni, Giovanni. 2017. A First Course in Sobolev Spaces. American Mathematical Soc. https://www.google.co.uk/books/edition/A_First_Course_in_Sobolev_Spaces/qoA8DwAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=0.
———. 2023. A First Course in Fractional Sobolev Spaces. Vol. 229. American Mathematical Society. https://www.google.co.uk/books/edition/A_First_Course_in_Fractional_Sobolev_Spa/lh2_EAAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=giovanni+leoni+fractional+sobolev&pg=PP1&printsec=frontcover.
Savaré, Giuseppe. 1998. “Regularity Results for Elliptic Equations in Lipschitz Domains.” Journal of Functional Analysis 152 (1): 176–201. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002212369793158X/pdf?md5=c646200fe7117dd7d25d27439f36b342&pid=1-s2.0-S002212369793158X-main.pdf.
Sawano, Yoshihiro et al. 2018. Theory of Besov Spaces. Vol. 56. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-981-13-0836-9.
Stein, Elias M. 1970. Singular Integrals and Differentiability Properties of Functions. Princeton university press. https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9781400883882/html.
Taylor, Michael. 2013. Partial Differential Equations II: Qualitative Studies of Linear Equations. Vol. 116. Springer Science & Business Media.
Triebel, Hans. 1992. Theory of Function Spaces II. Vol. 84. Birkhäuser. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-0346-0419-2.
