item.title: PDES 4: A physical derivation of parabolic and elliptic PDE
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Summary

Partial differential equations (PDEs) are a fundamental tool that can be used to describe the evolution and stationary state of a physical system. These PDEs can be derived by understanding the processes that cause the “mass” of the system to vary via a balance equation. Namely diffusion, advection, reaction and sources.

2 Notation

Following the convention in fluid mechanics, vectors in are written in bold to differentiate them from scalars in . Given and we write the gradient and divergence as

For example, the Laplacian is equal to .

3 Introduction

Welcome to the fourth post on our series on PDEs. In previous posts, we built up the theory of function spaces necessary to address the fundamental problems of these equations. However, before we dive into more mathematical waters, it is convenient to get a sense of where these equations come from and what each term within the PDEs means. This can help us understand more deeply the equations and motivates the theory to follow.

We begin by giving a physical derivation of the parabolic equation

and its stationary version

by calculating the rate of change of the “mass” of the system in terms of its flow. Then, we introduce boundary effects and wrap up with some examples.

4 A physical derivation

Warning, proceed with caution: the following section contains a physical derivation of (1). As a result, some physical intuition and approximate reasoning is used. All functions are supposed smooth and integrable as needed. This disclaimer out of the way, let’s consider a spatial domain filled with fluid in which some solute is dissolved. Our goal is to describe the concentration (density) of the solute as the system evolves in time and space. We know that the amount of fluid within any subregion is

This mass changes as the solute moves around . By conservation of mass, the mass of solute at a small instant of time later is equal to the mass present at time plus the mass of any other solute that entered the domain in that small time

The solute can only enter if there is some external source, such as a pipe adding a mass of solute at point , or by flowing its boundary . We now consider this second case. Let describe the magnitude and velocity of the flow (flux) of the solute and consider a point on the boundary. If the flux is orthogonal to the outward pointing unit normal at (that is, tangent to at ), no fluid enters through . Whereas if the flux is parallel to , all of the flow at enters if is pointed in the opposite or leaves if and have the same direction. Otherwise, we get something in between, depending on the angle that and form. This situation can be described as follows

where is the dimensional Hausdorff measure (surface measure) and the minus sign means that if the solute is flowing in the same direction as , mass decreases, and if it flows in the opposite direction, mass increases, as points outwards. Rearranging terms and taking limits when goes to zero gives

Now, the mass of the solute in is just the integral of the density over . Using this and the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divergence_theorem#:~:text=

This is the integral form of the balance equation. To obtain the non-integral form, note that, since (2) holds for all , the integrands must be equal (almost) everywhere, that is

We would now like to express the flux in terms of the properties of the fluid and domain. We recall that determines the magnitude and direction of the flow of the solute. We distinguish two possible reasons for the movement of the solute.

  1. Diffusion: This is the process that causes the solute to move from areas of lower to higher concentration. A possible physical approximation is to consider the diffusion to be proportional to the gradient of the density, that is

    Here, is called the diffusivity, diffusion coefficient or viscosity depending on the context and is a positive definite matrix. The diffusivity encodes the preference of the solute to flow in one direction or another depending on the properties of the domain itself. If has orthonormal eigensystem

    Then

    That is, the solute diffuses in the direction of with speed proportional to . For example, if is a constant multiple of the identity, there is no preferred direction of flow. In this case, one says that the flow is homogeneous. The minus sign in (4) together with the imposition that is positive definite means that diffusion occurs from areas of lower to higher concentration.

    If diffusion is the only cause of movement in the fluid, and substituting into the balance equation (3) gives the (non-homogeneous) heat equation

  2. Advection: Another possible cause for the flow of the solute within is that the fluid itself is moving with some velocity , transporting along the particles of the solute. The flux due to advection is

The flux is thus made up of the sum of a diffusion and advection component:

Substituting this into the balance equation (3) gives

Finally, we may have a change in the concentration of the solute due to the solute reacting with another substance. For example, could be the density of a contaminant which we are eliminating from the fluid via a chemical process. Alternatively, could be a radioactive substance which is decaying. The reaction term is typically denoted by . In the simplest case, is linear in , equal to where the sign of determines whether the concentration of solute decreases (if , as in the previous scenarios) or increases (if , for example, could represent the concentration of a population of algae). Adding this reaction term to our balance equation (5) gives

where all terms are functions of . Applying the chain rule we may decompose

The first summand represents the transport of the solute due to the movement of the fluid, and the second the transport due to the contraction of the fluid, if , or its expansion, if . If , the fluid neither expands nor compresses and is called incompressible. In any case, writing (for notational consistency) gives

Equation (6) is a prototypical parabolic equation. Suppose now that our system has and reaches an equilibrium state (a state in which the concentration of solute stays constant in time once reached). Then and we obtain

Equations (6), and (7) are, respectively, the parabolic and elliptic PDE we were aiming for and, as we have just seen, each of their parts has a precise physical meaning in terms of diffusion, advection, reaction and source.

5 Boundary conditions

In an application, the system we are studying will evolve within some smooth bounded domain . In order for a unique solution to be defined it is necessary to impose a boundary condition for what is allowed to do on (as well as an initial condition in the parabolic case (6)).

Example 1 Consider the Poisson equation

If solves (8), then also solves (8) where is any harmonic function (i.e., ), such as a polynomial of degree 0 or 1.

There are multiple types of boundary conditions which can be specified, each one corresponding to a particular behaviour of the system.

  1. Dirichlet boundary condition: This is the additional imposition that

    where is some function defined on and is restricted to through the trace theorem developed in the previous post. In the context of the diffusion of heat, could be a rod which is kept at a constant temperature at its endpoints.

  2. Robin boundary condition: Here, it is imposed that

    where is the flux and in our case is . This condition imposes that a “mass” of substance (solute, heat, etc.) enters the domain at each point of the boundary (or leaves if the minus in (9) is omitted).

  3. Neumann boundary condition:This is a particular case of the Robin boundary condition where there is no advection flux at the boundary. In this case, (9) becomes

    The above is known as a Neumann boundary condition. If the material is homogeneous, that is, , the special notation

    is used. Here is known as the normal derivative.

  4. Mixed boundary condition: This corresponds a mix of the preceding. That is, is partitioned into , and the following boundary conditions are imposed.

  5. Periodic boundary conditions: Here the domain is an interval and we require that for all

    Equivalently, is a function on the torus , where the lattice is . These boundary conditions are typically used to approximate a system evolving on a very large domain by working only with a representative cell .

Finally, mathematically, it also makes sense to work with infinite domains such as the whole Euclidean space . In this case, rather than a boundary condition, one imposes suitable decay on the function such as or its derivatives such as .

We end the post by commenting that non-linear terms may be considered in the PDE (6)-(7). Many examples can be found here. However, the mathematical theory of nonlinear PDE is much more complicated (think Navier Stokes). The linear case, which we begin to develop in the next post, will keep us busy for a while.

6 References

item.title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • Given open the Sobolev spaces are complete spaces of ** weakly differentiable** functions.

  • Smooth functions are dense in if is bounded and has boundary. This lets us manipulate Sobolev functions as if they were smooth and then take limits. In particular, we can extend and restrict functions to .

  • Functions in enjoy various inequalities. Cashing in differentiability for integrability we can compactly embed

    Where decreases with the dimension , increases with differentiability and integrability , and is larger than .

2 Why should I care?

Sobolev spaces allow us to extend the notion of differentiability to a wider class of functions. The fact that these spaces are complete and compactly embedded in spaces is an important tool to extract convergent sub-sequences. This is useful when solving differential equations as a common technique is to take a Cauchy sequence whose limit is the solution to the equation.

3 Notation

  • In this post we will be dealing with functions over a variety of domains. To facilitate interpretation of the notation we will stick to the convention that is a compact set, are open sets, and is an open bounded set with boundary.

  • Given a topological space and a subset we abbreviate is dense in with the topology of by

    We stress that in practice may be itself a subset of some larger space (for example and ) . However, the above notation will always mean the closure with the topology of and not .

  • A related notation is we will write given

    To mean the limit in the topology of .

  • Given two sets we write and say that is compactly included in if is compact and strictly included in .

  • We also write

  • Given a topological vector space we write for the dual of and denote given the duality pairing as

  • We will always write for a multi-index and use the notation

    In the case we use the convention .

  • Given a space of functions with domain and we write

  • Given two quantities we write to mean that there exists some constant independent of and such that .

  • We write for the ball centered at with radius and if . The space where the ball is contained depending on context.

4 Introduction

Hi everyone, welcome back to another post on our series on PDEs. This post goes into some depth on the main space of functions we will work with. Since the post is rather long (yes it has appendices, no that wasn’t planned) I decided to include a pdf at the end of the post in case it is easier to navigate. That said, in the future we will want to solve a differential equation of the form

Where is some domain in . In a previous post on the Fourier transform we saw how to define the Sobolev spaces when is the whole Euclidean space or the torus . These spaces correspond to -times weakly differentiable functions and we saw how these spaces could help us solve (1). However, in practice may be an open set in or even some -dimensional manifold with a boundary condition

Note that equation (2) is a priori ill-defined as the Lebesgue measure of is zero. Thus, it is necessary to extend the theory to a wider class of domains and to explain what we mean by the restriction of a function to its boundary of definition.

4.1 A first attempt

Suppose for example is an open set and . Then, we can try to define using our knowledge of by:

  1. Extending by zero outside of to form

  2. Studying if . That is, as we saw in the previous post, checking if

  3. Saying that if and only if .

However, this runs into problems as is shown in the following example:

Example 1 Let and take defined to be identically equal to . Note that so we expect that for all . However, it holds that with

As we can see, by substituting in our naive definition of gives that if and only if . Thus, our program of extending by zero and studying the regularity of the extension is not going to work. The reason for this is that, by extending by zero we introduce a discontinuity on at the boundary of .

4.2 A second approach

Alternatively, we could also define

As we will see later (Exercise 20) this is a better approach. However, it is not optimal as it requires some conditions on . For example, if is not smooth we cannot assert that .

5 Test functions and distributions

5.1 Seminorms and their topologies

We need a new approach. Motivated as in the previous post by duality we should begin by defining what is meant by a weak derivative of a function in . If are smooth functions then we have by integration by parts

In the previous post, we used that

  • If we can take as our test functions and use that multiplied by any function in ( and its derivatives) vanish at infinity to get rid of the boundary effects.

  • If we can take as our test functions as the boundary effect of periodic functions cancels out.

To obtain this cancellation on a general open we need to impose that our test function vanishes in a neighborhood of the boundary. That is we need our test functions to have compact support.

Definition 1 Let be an open set, then we define to be the space of smooth functions whose support is some compact set .

Another notation for is and it is often called the space of test functions for reasons we will later see. Given a compact subset we define for each

Definition 2 Given a compact subset of an open set we define

We endow with the topology generated by the countable family of seminorms

Later we will need to generate topologies when the family of seminorms is uncountable.

Definition 3 Let be a vector space and let be a family of seminorms on . Then we define , the topology generated by ,

That is, is a sub-basis of . Equivalently, is open if and only if it is an arbitrary union of finite intersections.

The above topology is equivalent to the initial topology generated by the family

We include some exercises to help the reader get more used to the topology that arises. I recommended trying to solve them for a few minutes before checking the hints.

Exercise 1 Write for the topology generated by , Show that is the coarsest topology that makes into a topological vector space (TVS).

TipHint

The fact that is a TVS follows from the triangle inequality and homogeneity of seminorms. The fact that it is the coarsest that makes continuous is that must be an open neighbourhood of the origin and in a TVS, by continuity of the sum, translation of an open set must be open.

Exercise 2 Show that is locally convex. That is, every point has a local basis of convex sets

TipHint

Show that is convex.

Exercise 3 Show that the topology is determined by the following property.

  • Given a <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_(mathematics)#:~:text=

TipHint

The topology of any topological space is completely determined by the convergence of nets. So it is enough to show that the property holds. The implication holds by the continuity of , the reverse follows from being able to fit into any basic set .

Exercise 4 Show that is complete and thus a <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fr

TipHint

The topology is metrizable as the family of seminorms is countable Use Exercise 3 to show that if is Cauchy then the sequence of derivatives converge uniformly to for all . It remains to show that

To do so use the fundamental theorem of calculus and induction.

Using as a stepping stone we can build a topology on . We use the approach in <https://terrytao.wordpress.com/2009/04/19/245c-notes-3-distributions/#:~:text=is \end{definition}

Exercise 5 Give an infinite restrictable family of seminorms.

TipHint

Valid answers include all the and norms.

Exercise 6 Show that with the smooth topology is a locally convex topological vector space (LCTVS).

TipHint

See Exercises Exercise 12

Exercise 7 (Smooth convergence is equal to local convergence) Show that if and only if there exists a compact set such that for all the support of and are in and

TipHint

Given any sequence and an increasing set of compact sets with show that

Is a restrictable seminorm. Why does this prevent the support of escaping to infinity? Now knowing all functions are supported in some use that is restrictable to conclude the proof.

Exercise 8 (Completeness) Show that with the smooth topology is complete.

TipHint

Given a Cauchy net show as in Exercise 7 that the support of cannot escape a compact set . Conclude using the completeness on and Exercise 3.

Exercise 9 Show that with the smooth topology is Hausdorff.

TipHint

The initial topology of a set of functions that separates points is Hausdorff.

Remark 1. The topology on is not metrizable (note the family of seminorms used to generate it is not countable) and as a result is not a Fréchet space. This and more can be found in (Leoni 2017) page 286.

The construction of the topology on is a bit technical a more intuitive construction would be to define the family of seminorms

And then define the topology on to be the one generated by this family of seminorms. This has the following problem.

Exercise 10 Show that with the topology generated by is not complete.

TipHint

Construct a sequence that is Cauchy with respect to every whose support escapes to infinity.

Using the smooth topology we can now work with the dual of

Definition 4 We define the space of distributions to be

And give it the weak- topology.

Remark 2. The inclusions

are continuous. As a result, . That is, distributions are a larger or more general class than tempered distributions.

Of course, not all smooth functions have compact support. Other (in this case Fréchet) spaces of smooth functions are

Where, we give them respectively the topologies generated by and .

An equivalent characterization of (Taylor 2013, 116:241) is that, if and only if for all the operator defined by

is continuous on .

5.2 Locally integrable functions as distributions

In our post on the Fourier transform we saw that integrable functions could naturally be considered as tempered distributions. The analogous is true for distributions. In this case, since our test functions are compactly supported, the functions we pair them up with only need to be locally integrable. We recall the definition

Definition 5 Given we write for the space of locally integrable functions,

And endow it with the topology generated by the family of seminorms

Where are selected such that .

Note that for all .

Exercise 11 Show that is a Fréchet space.

TipHint

The topology is metrizable as the family of seminorms is countable. By Exercise 3 if is Cauchy then is Cauchy for all so converges to some . Show that where to conclude the proof.

Theorem 1 (Locally integrable functions as distributions) The mapping

Defined by

Is injective and continuous.

Proof. We begin by showing that . Firstly, the integral in (5) is finite as has compact support. Secondly, if we write for the support of , it holds that

Which, by our equivalent characterization of in (4) shows that . We now show that the identification is injective. That is, if

Then . Consider a compact set and write for the extension of by zero outside of . Clearly . Consider an approximation to the identity (see Appendix Section 12 ) and set

By the approximation and smoothing of Propositions Proposition 89 we obtain a bounded sequence with for large enough and such that

By the first part of the above, we may take a subsequence converging to almost everywhere, and by the second we may apply the dominated convergence theorem to obtain that

As a result, vanishes on . Since was any compact subset of and every open set can be written as a union of compact sets we conclude that as desired. The continuity of follows from the estimate

Where has support (remember we are considering the weak- topology on ).

Due to the above immersion, we will naturally consider as a subspace of . In particular, any subspaces of such as or can also be considered as distributions.

Exercise 12 Show that the is not closed in .

TipHint

Show that an approximation to the identity converges to a Dirac delta . However .

5.3 Support of a distribution

In the continuous case, the support of a function is well-defined as the smallest closed set outside of which the function is zero. However, when working with an equivalence class of functions the definition must be amended (consider for example the support of ). This is resolved by the following definitions.

Definition 6 We say that a distribution vanishes on if

And write

If a function vanishes on a collection of sets it also vanishes on their union, this extends to distributions.

Lemma 1 Let be a collection of open sets in and suppose that

Then vanishes on .

Proof. Let have support in . Then, by compactness, we can extract a finite covering of . Let be a partition of unity subordinate to (see Appendix Section 13). Then

Since was any test function supported in this concludes the proof.

By the just proved Lemma 1 we see that there is a largest set on which vanishes. As a result, we can make the following definition.

Definition 7 Let and let be the largest open set on which vanishes. Then, we define the support of as

Since is naturally included in we obtain in particular the definition of support of a function .

Exercise 13 If the support of is the complementary of the largest open set on which is almost everywhere. In particular, if is continuous, the (distributional) support of coincides with the classical support of .

TipHint

We saw in Theorem 1 that is almost everywhere on some open set if and only if it integrates to against any test function on the open set. This shows the first part and the second follows immediately.

6 Sobolev spaces

Now that we have built the space of distributions we can define weak derivatives of test functions just as we did with tempered distributions.

Definition 8 Given we define the (weak) -th derivative by

Exercise 14 Show that .

TipHint

Show that is continuous on by using that are restrictable seminorms. Conclude by using that defined on is the adjoint of defined on

A prerequisite for the definition to make sense is that the notion corresponds to that of classical derivative.

Exercise 15 Let have classical derivatives . Then .

TipHint

By definition of weak derivatives and the chain rule, we have the distributional equality

The result follows by the injectivity of .

Definition 9 (Sobolev spaces) Given an open set , and we define the Sobolev space

Where is identified as a subspace of by Theorem 1. We give the norm

That is, is the space of -times (weakly) differentiable functions with derivatives in . An equivalent norm that is also sometimes used is

The local Sobolev spaces are defined similarly, where we now only require that

And now generate the topology by local seminorms analogously to .

Theorem 2 (Completeness of Sobolev spaces) For all and both and are Banach spaces. If these spaces are also reflexive.

Proof. Let be a Cauchy sequence in . Then, by definition of the norm on the sequence of derivatives is Cauchy in for each and since is complete, converge to some functions

To conclude, it suffices to show that verifies

This holds as, for every test function

Where the first and last inequality follows from the continuous immersion of in (Hölder’s inequality). Reflexivity follows from the fact that the mapping

Is an isometry, so is closed in the reflexive Banach space and thus reflexive (see (Brezis and Brézis 2011) page 70). The case of is proved identically now working with the local seminorms.

We now show some relevant properties of the weak derivative all of which are to be expected knowing the classical case. These can be greatly generalized with tools we will later develop.

Proposition 1 (Properties) Let . Then it holds that

  1. Leibniz rule: given it holds that

  2. For , the translation with .

  3. Let and . Then

Proof. Points 1 and 2 follow by the definition of weak derivative and the relevant properties for classical derivatives. The third property follows from the second as, by Fubini,

A natural question is what relationship there is between Sobolev functions and classical derivatives. For example, in dimension a classical result is that if and only if is absolutely continuous and has derivative almost everywhere. A more general result is as follows.

Proposition 2 (Absolute continuity on lines) The following are equivalent

  • .

  • There exists a representative of in that is almost everywhere differentiable with classical derivatives and, given it holds that this representative is absolutely continuous on almost all (with respect to the Lebesgue measure on ) line segments in parallel to the coordinate axis.

The above holds true if we replace with their none local counterparts .

We omit the proof which can be found in (Kinnunen 2023) pages . The next exercise show that, perhaps somewhat unexpectedly, to have it is not sufficient to require that is differentiable almost everywhere with integrable derivatives.

Example 2 The devil’s staircase is differentiable almost everywhere with .

As a result, if then would be constant (which it is not). In fact, as it is not absolutely continuous.

7 Smooth approximation of Sobolev functions

The definition of weak derivative requires one to integrate against smooth functions whenever trying to prove some property holds. This is somewhat cumbersome. One would much rather

  1. Work pretending all Sobolev functions are (classically) smooth.

  2. Manipulate them according to the standard rules of calculus.

  3. Obtain a result that holds for all Sobolev functions (and not just the classically smooth ones).

This process can be rigorously justified by the density of various spaces of smooth functions in Sobolev spaces. Or to use Tao’s terminology, by giving ourselves an epsilon of room.

In this section, we prove the relevant results. These rely heavily on the analogous density results for functions in (see Appendix Section 12). As a result, they will not when . We start without making any assumptions on and obtain two local-type results. Throughout this section we will often be switching between different open sets and, if following the proofs, making some drawings is recommended.

Theorem 3 (Local approximation by smooth functions) Let for , denote its extension by zero and let be an approximation to unity. Then

As a result, for any ,

Proof. Given a compactly embedded set we can take large enough so that and as a result, the convolution verifies (see Observation Remark 6)

Thus, by the third property Proposition 1, for all

Taking limits we conclude from Proposition 8 that

To conclude density of in consider such that

And a bump function that is equal to on and is supported in (this is possible by Uryshon’s lemma and a convolution). Then,

The density of in follows by taking converging to and as then, for every compactly included

This concludes the proof as by Exercise 3 local convergence is equivalent to convergence on every compactly included subset.

Remark 3. Note that, without further assumptions on it is impossible to get a global approximation by smooth functions defined on all of . This is because, the convolution can only be defined on

The above issue disappears when . This shows that

Theorem 4 (Global approximation in ) It holds that for all ,

Proof. Let be equal to on and set . Then, given and a smooth approximation to unity we obtain that, by the triangle inequality

The following Theorem shows a global-type approximation.

Theorem 5 (Meyers-Serrin: local till boundary approximation) Let be open, then for all

Proof. The proof is an instructive way of using a partition of unity to piece together a local result (in this case Theorem 3) to get a global one. Let and consider an open covering of with and . By Theorem 17 we may obtain a partition of unity subordinate to the “rings” (the trickery with the indices is so that the actually cover ). Where we relabel so that

We have that with so by the local approximation in Theorem 3 we can find such that

Now we obtain the global approximation by taking

As then

Note that Theorem 5 is not strictly stronger than Theorem 3 as it is not, in general, possible to extend functions in to . As a corollary of Meyers-Serrin’s theorem, we obtain an equivalent definition of .

Exercise 16 Let be an open set and . Then is equal to the completion of with the norm.

TipHint

Use that is complete and that the completion of a metric space is unique.

Before Theorem 5 was proved, both our original definition Definition 9 (distributions with derivatives in ) and the one in Exercise 16 (closure of smooth functions with Sobolev norm) were used as the definition of . But it was unclear which was the “correct” Sobolev space. This debate was settled by Meyers and Serrin who proved that, as we just showed, both are equal.

We now show an example of how these kinds of density results can be useful. The following generalizes the second point of Proposition 1

Exercise 17 (Change of variables) Let be open in and be bijective with . Then for any it holds that and the usual chain rule holds. For example

TipHint

Give yourself an of room. By induction, it suffices to consider the case .We can approximate on each compact by a sequence of functions . For the equality Equation 6 holds. Furthermore, by a change of variables, (6) is continuous in so we may pass to the limit and obtain (6) for on . Since was any, the equality also holds on the whole of .

In Exercise 17 it is important that is diffeomorphic so that composition with is continuous.

Exercise 18 Show that the conclusion of Exercise 17 is false if we only assume that and do not impose invertibility.

TipHint

Divide by zero.

Now we provide a final global approximation result in the case where the domain is smooth (see Appendix Section 14 for a review on manifolds with boundary) and bounded.

Theorem 6 (Global, smooth on boundary approximation) Let be a bounded open domain with boundary, then for all .

Proof. The idea will be to locally translate points close to the boundary further into so that we can convolve with an approximation of unity and then recover the global case with a partition of unity. We begin by “straightening the boundary”. That is, since is , given there exists an open set and a function such that, relabeling and flipping the last coordinate axis if necessary

By considering a translation in the last coordinate and its mollification by an approximation of unity

For big enough we have that is well defined and smooth on . Since translation is continuous on for and by the behavior of differentiation with convolution (see Proposition 1), for large enough

Now, since is bounded is compact we may extract a finite covering of and functions smooth on such that

Now we take an open set such that cover . By the local approximation of Theorem 3 we know we can approximate on by some

Finally, we take a smooth partition of unity subordinate to and a bump function which is equal to on and is supported on (see Example 5) and set

This gives the desired approximation

This concludes the proof.

In contrast to Theorem 5, Theorem 6 shows that Sobolev functions on smooth bounded domains can be approximated by functions that are also smooth on the boundary of the domain . This will prove fundamental in the next section. Both to extend them to the whole of and to restrict them to .

8 Extensions and restrictions

Many results in the theory of Sobolev spaces are proved first on and then extended to more general domains by a continuous extension followed by a restriction. For example, if one can show that for some then, denoting by the extension of to

which shows that . In the reasoning above, it is necessary for the extension to be continuous, otherwise we fail on the first . As we saw previously, merely extending by zero will not do and in this section, we develop the necessary tools to extend Sobolev functions from a domain to .

Definition 10 Let be a topological space of functions for each open . We say that is an extension domain for if there exists a continuous operator

such that for almost all . We call an extension operator.

For an extension to be possible, it is crucial that is regular enough. It will be sufficient to impose that have uniformly Lipschitz boundary. The following definition and Theorem can be found in (Leoni 2017, sec. 13).

Definition 11 We say that an open set has uniformly Lipschitz boundary if there exists and an open covering of such that

  1. For all there exists a such that .

  2. Every point in is contained in at most sets .

  3. For each there exist local coordinates and a Lipschitz continuous function (both depending on ), with Lipschitz constant , such that , where is given in local coordinates by

That is, a domain is uniformly Lipschitz if it has a locally finite covering which makes it of type (see Appendix Section 14). Below we show two typical examples.

Exercise 19 Show that if is bounded with Lipschitz boundary or if then it has uniformly Lipschitz boundary.

TipHint

For the first part, use the fact that is bounded to extract a finite subcovering. For the second use that and that everything is true for elements of the empty set.

The following theorem shows that if is uniformly Lipschitz, is an extension domain for . To simplify the proof we will give the proof in the case of bounded open sets with boundary. Using the approximation of Sobolev functions by functions smooth on the boundary and a change of variables, we can extend functions in to the whole of .

Theorem 7 (General extension) Let be an open set with uniformly Lipschitz boundary. Then is an extension domain for and for all .

Proof. We prove the result when the domain is bounded with boundary and when . The general result can be found in (Leoni 2017, 424).

We work first in the upper half-space (the canonical example of a manifold with boundary)

That is, we suppose that there is some open set such that

Where

By Theorem 6 we also give ourselves an epsilon of room by supposing that . We define the extension of to as

We will have as long as we can get the derivatives to match up on the boundary . That is, as long as verify

The above is a system of equations with -unknowns . Its matrix is the Vandermonde matrix (which is invertible). As a result, the system may be solved to extend . By the form of we have the bound

Where . Working now in the general case for , we may cover the compact by a finite amount of bounded open sets such that

Where are open in and are diffeomorphisms. By the previous case, we can extend to functions and then transform back to the original space to get

Where

The hidden constant depending only on . Next, using a partition of unity subordinate to we glue the local extensions together to form an extension to all of

The desired extension can now be obtained by multiplying with a bump function that is supported in and equal to on .

By (7) we have that

As a result, is a (bounded) linear operator. So far we had considered . Now, since by Theorem 6 the space is dense in we may extend to a linear operator on the whole of . A verification shows that is also bounded as an operator from to . This concludes the proof.

Exercise 20 (Restriction) Under the conditions of ?@thm-extension it holds that

That is, functions in are equal to the restriction of functions in respectively.

TipHint

Using extensions also gives us a way to define the Sobolev spaces when the exponent is real valued.

Definition 12 Given a bounded open set with boundary of type with , we define for all real

To further generalize this definition to domains where restriction is not possible one needs to use complex interpolation (see for example (Taylor 2013) pages 321-333).

9 Trace theorem

As we already discussed, a PDE often incorporates boundary information such as . This is well defined if is continuous, however, if , and is thus only defined almost everywhere, then is a priori not well defined. The following theorem remedies this issue.

Proposition 3 (Trace) Let be open with Lipschitz boundary and let . Then there exists a continuous linear operator

Such that for all .

Proof. We will only prove the case where is bounded and of class . The proof of the general statement can once more be found in (Leoni 2017, 592).

As in previous results, the trick is to suppose first is smooth, work locally, and then obtain a global result using a partition of unity and the density in Theorem 6. \

Given we take a open set containing . Flattening out the boundary by where necessarily the boundary is preserved

and using the extension ?@thm-extension to extend to with compact support we obtain by the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divergence_theorem#:~:text=space

Where in the second inequality we used the chain rule and in the last Hölder’s inequality. Since is and by the continuity of the extension we obtain what we are looking for in

We had supposed smooth, now taking a finite covering of (this is possible by compactness of ) and taking a subordinate partition of unity we conclude from (8) that

That is,

Using Theorem 6 to extend continuously to concludes the proof.

Note that, if is bounded, then so .

Definition 13 We define the trace of as . We also use the notation

To get an estimate on the trace we paid -degree of regularity. We can do better and only pay degrees of regularity. This uses the theory of <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trace_operator#For_p_=_1:~:text=

Theorem 8 For all real the trace operator is a continuous operator

Proof. Straightening out the boundary and using the extension operator as in the trace Proposition 3, it is sufficient to work in the case where is smooth and defined on . By Fourier inversion, if we write

So, by Cauchy-Schwarz

The change of variables shows that

Where in the inequality it was used that . We deduce from (9) and (10) on taking norms that

Which concludes the proof.

A particularly useful space of functions related to the trace operator is the following

Definition 14 We define the space of functions with trace zero as

Where the closure is with respect to the topology on .

Exercise 21 Show that for all

Use the continuity of the trace operator.

The converse to Exercise 21 holds but is far from trivial.

Proposition 4 Let be a bounded open set with boundary. Then

The proof is very technical, see (Evans 2022) page 274 for the details.\

Being able to approximate functions in by smooth functions compactly supported inside of gives us many more tools. For example, a function in can be extended by zero to obtain an element in even for non-smooth unbounded domains.

Exercise 22 (Extension trace 0) Let be an open set, and define

Then

is a linear with .

Exercise 23 (Integration by parts 1) Let be any open set and consider then

10 Sobolev embeddings and inequalities

Sobolev inequalities are relationships that bound the norm of in different function spaces depending on how differentiable and integrable is. For example, such a relationship could look like

By considering the rescaling , performing a change of variables, and taking to we see that for such a relationship to hold it is necessary that

The case gives rise to the following definition

Definition 15 The Sobolev conjugate of is defined by

Note that . The idea behind inequalities such as (11) is to cash in some differentiability for some integrability. The main results used to do this are based on the fundamental theorem of calculus. First, we need the following lemma.

Lemma 2 (Loomis-Whitney inequality) Let , let for some , and define

Then,

Proof. The case is immediate by Fubini. The general case follows from induction on . We write

Where in the first inequality we applied Cauchy-Schwarz with . Now applying the general version of Hölder’s inequality

to gives

Substituting into (14) concludes the proof.

Using the Loomis-Whitney inequality and the fundamental theorem of calculus gives us our first Sobolev inequality

Theorem 9 (Sobolev-Gagliardo-Niremberg) Given it holds that

Proof. By density (see Theorem 4), it is enough to take . Applying the fundamental theorem of calculus gives

Multiplying all these inequalities together gives
