This holds if and only if . Otherwise, we require more integrability. However, this extra integrability can be converted into regularity in the converse direction of Sobolev spaces. First, we recall the following definition

Definition 16 Let be open and , we define the Hölder space

Where the Holder norm is defined as

For the is the space of functions with bounded derivatives up to order and whose -th order derivatives are Lipschitz continuous.

Theorem 11 (Morrey) Let and set . Then, the following inclusion is continuous

The proof is technical and can be found in (Brezis and Brézis 2011) page 282.

As is logical, as approaches from above the extra differentiability we get goes to . Furthermore, no matter how much integrability we cash in, we can never get more differentiability than we started with, so as .

Exercise 27 (Sobolev regularity) Let and . Set and . Then, the following inclusion is continuous

TipHint

By iteratively applying the continuous Sobolev embedding times, we obtain a chain of continuous inclusions: where the exponents are governed by the relation So by assumption we verify the Morrey Condition . As a result, with .

We started with derivatives, spent to reach integrability , and to apply Morrey’s inequality and obtain regularity. The number of classical derivatives remaining is .

In the edge case where is an integer, the bootstrapping argument stops at , which embeds into for all , but fails to embed into and . To guarantee continuity, we must sacrifice to a slightly lower integrability in our final bootstrapping step. This pushes us back into the Morrey regime where , yielding , meaning we achieve for all .

Combining the three results gives

Theorem 12 (Rellich-Kondrachov) Let be bounded with boundary, then the following inclusions are continuous

Where is defined by the relation , , and or if . Furthermore, the first for , second, fourth, and third composed with fourth inclusions are compact.

Exercise 28 Show that if is unbounded then the inclusions in Theorem 12 are not compact.

TipHint

Consider a sequence of smooth functions whose support goes to infinity. For example, for consider the sequence where .

Proof. The fact that the above inclusions are continuous follows by our three Sobolev inequalities in Exercises Exercise 25,Exercise 26,Exercise 27 together with the extension ?@thm-extension.

The compactness of the embeddings requires reduce to showing that the unit ball in each of the embedded spaces is equicontinuous.

  1. For the first inclusion we will use <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fr

    Where the above is known to be true for smooth functions by the fundamental theorem of calculus and extends by density to (we recall translation is continuous on for ). The above also holds for and since we can write

    By <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz

    This shows equicontinuity and concludes the first case.

  2. The compactness of the second inclusion is proved identically.

  3. For the compactness of the last inclusion we use <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arzel

    As a result, for each the family

    is equicontinuous. So by Arzelà–Ascoli we may extract a sequence such that converges uniformly to some . By the fundamental theorem of calculus we conclude that and as a result . That is, the unit ball is sequentially compact and thus compact when embedded in . This proves this point.

  4. The composition of the inclusions on the last line is compact as the composition of a compact and a continuous operator is compact. This concludes the proof.

As a particular case of the above we obtain the following simpler looking corollary

Corollary 1 Let , then it holds that following inclusion is compact

In particular, for all , the following inclusion is compact

Proof. For any of the cases in Rellich-Kondrachov’s Theorem 12, the condition on holds. The first part of the result then follows as and the second part from the fact that .

Compact embeddings in spaces of higher order can be obtained through the following.

Proposition 5 (Higher degree embeddings) Let and be such that

is compact. Then, the inclusion

is also compact.

Proof. Let be a bounded sequence. By assumption is compact in , so we may extract a subsequence such that, for ,

Using the same technique as in Theorem 2 shows that . This shows that in and, by the equivalence of compactness and sequential compactness, concludes the proof.

This result can be combined directly with Rellich-Kondrachov Theorem 12 or any other embedding. For example, when combined with Corollary 1 we get

Corollary 2 Let , then it holds that following inclusion is compact

In particular, for all , the following inclusion is compact

Using Remark 4 we can also obtain Sobolev embeddings for non-smooth domains if we consider Sobolev spaces with trace .

Theorem 13 (Rellich for trace 0 and irregular domain) Let be a bounded domain. Then, the conclusions of Theorem 12, higher degree embeddings, and its corollary hold if we swap everywhere for and for .

Proof. The proof follows in an identical method as the aforementioned results as we still have density of smooth functions and the extension theorem, which is now given by Exercise 22.

The main utility of all these compact embeddings is that given a sequence whose derivatives are bounded in certain norms we can extract convergent subsequences in appropriate spaces. We end this post (modulo appendices) with one of the most useful inequalities which we will make use of in future posts.

Theorem 14 (Poincaré inequality) Let where is bounded in one direction. Then

Proof. By density (which holds by definition of ) of it is sufficient to reason for and pass to the limit. By relabeling we may suppose is bounded along the axis. That is, for some finite

The idea is to use the fundamental theorem of calculus together with the compact support of . This allows us to rewrite

Taking norms above gives

This concludes the proof.

11 Convolutions and regularization

The convolution of two functions can be thought of as “blurring” by averaging it against . In the case where is smooth, this blurring has the effect of smoothing out any sharp edges and irregularities in . This allows us to approximate irregular functions by smooth ones and serves as an important technical tool in our analysis.

Definition 17 (Convolution of functions) Given and we define the convolution of to be the function

The definition given by (18) is purposefully vague. We still need to specify what spaces belong to so that is a well-defined element (of a further unspecified space). This can be done as follows.

Proposition 6 (Young’s convolution inequality) Consider the definition in (18) and let . Then it holds that

  1. If and then with

  2. Suppose that

    Then with

  3. In any of the above cases .

Proof. The first point follows from the triangle inequality for the Bochner integral (in this context this is also called Minkowski’s integral inequality) as

To see the second point fix and define the linear operator . Then, for and respectively

Where the first inequality is point one and the second follows from Cauchy-Schwarz. Now applying < https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz

The definition of convolution can be extended to even more settings, for example, suppose that is the density of some finite (possibly signed) measure and is bounded, then

Definition 18 (Convolution of function with measure) Let be a finite signed Borel measure on and then we define the convolution

Note that, once more by the triangle inequality, the convolution is well-defined with

Now, if we consider to be the densities of some finite (signed) measures then we obtain that for bounded

That is, the convolution of with is the pushforward of the product measure with the sum .

Definition 19 (Convolution of measures) Let be two finite signed measures on . Then the convolution of is the pushforward

The language of random variables can give some good motivation for this

Example 3 Let be random variables with law then has law . Furthermore, if are independent and are absolutely continuous with densities then is absolutely continuous with density .

Proof. The first part is by definition of pushforward. To show that has density it suffices to read the reasoning in equation (19) backward.

Through the random variable interpretation, we also see that if have all their mass in then their convolution must have all its mass in . That is,

Lemma 3 Let be such that the convolution is well-defined. Then

Proof. This follows directly from the definition of convolution.

One technical point is that to define the convolution of two objects it is required that they be defined globally. For example if , we can’t convolve with as the integral

requires we evaluate on all of . One workaround is, if with we can extend to be equal to some outside of

Then, the convolution is well defined and equal to

As we can see, the convolution in general depends on how we extend outside of . However, it is independent of the extension for in

With

For this reason, we will employ the following notation.

Definition 20 Given and with we define as

Convolution of distributions with test functions can also be considered. A similar reason to previously leads us to the following definition

Definition 21 Let and . Then we define the convolution by

In the above we can also swap all occurrences of and by and respectively. An interesting fact is that the convolution of a distribution with a function is itself a function.

Proposition 7 Convolution of distribution and test function is smooth:

  1. Let then .

  2. Let then .

The previous definitions all go through word by word in the case where we substitute the domain from Euclidean space to a LCA group with the Haar measure . For example

A typical case is when with the Lebesgue measure or with the counting measure. These respectively give

The same results are also obtained. In fact, save the commutation , the above results hold even if is not Abelian. In this case, one considers the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haar_measure#:~:text=

12 Smoothing in

In this section, we examine how convolution can be used to approximate functions by smoother ones. This is of great practical use as it allows us to

  1. Consider an appropriate space of functions for our problem, smooth or otherwise.

  2. Perform formal manipulations using the standard rules of calculus as if all functions in this space were smooth and compactly supported until we obtain a desired result.

  3. Pass to the limit to recover the expression for the whole class of functions.

A crucial tool in this program is the following:

Definition 22 We say that a family of functions is an approximation to unity if

  • Norm 1: .

  • Decreasing support: .

If we say that are smooth.

Remark 5. The above definition is frequently also given letting the index set range over and taking . This is equivalent and simply leads to taking instead of .

The first question is whether a smooth approximation to the identity exists. In the following example, we answer this in the affirmative.

Example 4 Let then

is a smooth approximation of the identity. Additionally,

By Proposition 6 is a Banach algebra. However, it is a non-unital one. That is there does not exist an element such that

However, we will soon see that in a limiting sense, an identity for the convolution exists. First, we need the following lemma.

Lemma 4 The space is dense in for all .

Proof. Consider . If for some measurable set with finite measure then, by the outer and inner regularity of the Lebesgue measure we may take open and compact respectively with and

Where we wrote for the Lebesgue measure on . By <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urysohn

Since the space of simple functions is dense in this concludes the proof.

The name “approximation of unity” in Definition Definition 22 is justified by the following proposition.

Proposition 8 Let where and consider and an approximation to unity . Then it holds that

Proof. Consider . Using that has mass and is supported on .

Now taking norms and large enough gives

Where in the last inequality we used that is uniformly continuous and has mass . Since was any, this shows the first part of the proposition.

We now prove the second part. By Lemma 4 we can choose such that

Now, since , whose measure is bounded by some , the inequality in (21) shows that

Now using the triangle inequality and Young’s convolution inequality Proposition 6 gives

This shows the second part and concludes the proof.

The question is why would we want to approximate a function by its convolutions with some smooth functions the answer is given in the following two results.

Proposition 9 (Smoothing effect) Let and . Then with

Furthermore, if is compactly supported then .

Proof. By induction, it suffices to consider the case for some . This case can be proved by a differentiation under the integral sign as, given

Where is the support of .

Remark 6 (Local smoothing). The smoothing effect also holds when is only defined on some open set . Then, with the notation of Definition Definition 20, with an identical proof showing

Theorem 15 It holds that

Where the closures are respectively in the and the uniform topology given by .

Proof. This follows immediately from Proposition 8 and Proposition 9.

Exercise 29 Let be an open subset of , show that

TipHint

Let be the non-smooth function to approximate, multiply it first by a mollifier equal to in (see Example 5). Convolve to get

Show as in Theorem 15 that converges appropriately.

The above can be generalized to non-Euclidean spaces

Theorem 16 Let be a measure space such that is locally convex and Hausdorff and is inner and outer regular. Then

Suppose additionally that is a group, that is the left or right Haar measure, and that there exists an approximation to unity on . Then

Proof. The first part can be proved by the same method as in Lemma 4 (note that Uryshon’s lemma still holds for LCH spaces). The second part follows by copying the proof of Propositions Proposition 8 and Proposition 9.

The assumption of the existence of an approximation of unity is perhaps the most delicate, but it can be applied for example in the following case.

Corollary 3 It holds that

Note that the second part of Corollary 3 also follows from the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stone Similar results hold in the space of distributions.

Proposition 10 Let and be an approximation to unity. Then

As a result, .

The proof can be found in (Leoni 2017) page 308.

13 Global to local and back again

Often it is advantageous to work locally and then reason in the general case by some kind of approximation. A useful tool in this respect are bump functions.

Definition 23 A bump function (also called cutoff function) is a function .

Constructing bump functions that have some desired support is a tool we use frequently throughout. Here we provide two examples to show how this may be done. Other constructions are of course possible.

Example 5 Given two open sets with there exists with support in , equal to on and with .

Proof. Since , there exists a compact with

Now, by <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urysohn

Example 6 There exists a sequence of such that

And .

Proof. Let be any (for example that of (20)). Then we can take .

In the opposite direction. One is often in the situation where it is possible to derive some local properties for a given object (think manifolds). To recover a global result one needs some way to piece together the local results. A useful tool in this respect is partitions of unity.

Definition 24 Given a manifold and an open covering of we say that is a partition of unity on if:

  1. for some .

  2. For each , it holds that for only a finite amount of .

Partitions of unity are often used in differential geometry as follows

  1. Work in some open subset (or the upper half space if our manifold has boundary) to prove the existence of some object with desired properties.

  2. Cover the manifold with coordinate charts and translate the euclidean result via the identification with to obtain locally defined .

  3. Obtain a globally defined object by using the partition of unity to piece together the local objects

In addition to the approximation and extension theorems in Section Section 8, partitions of unity can be used to show that: every manifold has a Riemannian metric, show that a function is smooth on some non-open set iff it is the restriction of a smooth function defined on a neighborhood of , prove the existence of an outward pointing vector on manifolds with boundary, define integration over an orientable manifold , prove Stokes’ theorem.

Theorem 17 Let be a smooth manifold (in particular we assume is Hausdorff), then every open covering has a partition of unity .

The proof is based on the existence of bump functions (something we have already proved for ) and is straightforward in the case that is compact. The general case can be reduced to the compact setting by obtaining a covering by relatively compact open sets of such that

And then working with the compact . See (Tu 2011) Appendix C for the details.

14 Manifolds with boundary

We will be defining differential equations on open domains . In this case will be a “manifold with boundary” whose regularity will determine what results we have access to. The prototypical example of a manifold with a boundary is the upper half space

Here the inequality is not strict so that the boundary of is included in itself. Since is not open we need to define what is meant by saying that a function is differentiable on such a set.

Definition 25 Let be an arbitrary set. We say that a function is -times differentiable at if there exists a function which is times differentiable at and such that on .

Definition 26 If is -times differentiable at every point of we say that .

Exercise 30 Show that if and only if there exists an extension of which is equal to on .

TipHint

Use a partition of unity.

A manifold with boundary is just a generalization of , where we impose that is “locally equal” to . That is, there exists a covering of by open sets and a collection of homeomorphisms with the subspace topology from

And where for compatibility we impose that for each the functions

are diffeomorphisms on (again with the subspace topology). Here is an atlas of . We say that is if the diffeomorphisms in (23) are (see Definition Definition 25).

Definition 27 We say that is a manifold with boundary if is a second countable Hausdorff space and is a atlas.

The boundary of is its points that are mapped to the boundary in .

Definition 28 Given a point we say that is a boundary point if for some (and thus every chart) . We call the set of all boundary points the boundary of and denote it by .

Exercise 31 Show that the restricted atlas makes a dimensional manifold without boundary.

TipHint

By definition of boundary

And the coordinate changes are as the restriction of a map is .

In our case we will always take to be a subset of , in this case, different variations of the above definition are possible. For example, by the inverse function theorem and Exercise 30, we can extend the functions to diffeomorphisms on open in so that (22)-(24) now read

Additionally, by the implicit function theorem there exists for each coordinate set functions such that, relabeling the coordinates and decreasing the size of if necessary,

Let us write , by the Taylor expansion

We deduce that, once more reducing the size of if necessary and depending on the sign of on , one and only one of the following two hold

The equivalent formulations in (25) and in (26)-(27) are used in the main exposition. Finally, in our case, we will typically take where is some open set. In this case, we adopt the following terminology.

Definition 29 We say that an open set has boundary of is a manifold with boundary.

In the above case, the topological and manifold boundaries of necessarily coincide as homeomorphisms map topological boundaries to topological boundaries.

15 References

Brezis, Haim, and Haim Brézis. 2011. Functional Analysis, Sobolev Spaces and Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 2. 3. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-0-387-70914-7.
Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.
Kinnunen, Juha. 2023. Sobolev Spaces. https://math.aalto.fi/~jkkinnun/files/sobolev_spaces.pdf.
Leoni, Giovanni. 2017. A First Course in Sobolev Spaces. American Mathematical Soc. https://bookstore.ams.org/gsm-105#:~:text=A%20First%20Course%20in%20Sobolev%20Spaces&text=Sobolev%20spaces%20are%20a%20fundamental,BV%20functions%20of%20one%20variable.
Taylor, Michael. 2013. Partial Differential Equations II: Qualitative Studies of Linear Equations. Vol. 116. Springer Science & Business Media.
Tu, Loring W. 2011. “Manifolds.” In An Introduction to Manifolds. Springer. https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4419-7400-6_3.

item.title: PDEs 2: What is a Hilbert space?
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • Hilbert spaces are Banach spaces with an inner product. They correspond to an infinite dimensional version of or .

  • Much of Euclidean geometry carries over to Hilbert spaces including orthogonality, projections, orthonormal basis, and Pythagoras’ theorem.

  • Compact operators are a simple kind of operator that can be approximated by infinite dimensional ones.

2 Why should I care?

Hilbert spaces come with beautiful geometry and give us tools that are not available in ordinary Banach spaces. These allow us to prove the existence of infinite dimensional equations and even pose them as minimization problems. When you can use a Hilbert space to analyze your problem your life is always a lot easier.

3 Notation

Given we will write for the conjugate of .

Given a subset of some topological space, it is also common to write for the closure of . Though this is a slight abuse of notation we will do the same as the meaning will always be clear from context.

Given two topological vector spaces we write for the space of continuous linear operators from to and for the space of continuous linear functions to the base field of .

4 Introduction

In writing the next post in the series of PDEs I realized that there were many results from functional analysis which had not been discussed and would be necessary. As such, this post is meant as a short, yet rather longer than I intended, introduction to the basic theory that will be required.

I’m going to assume you know nothing (please don’t feel insulted) and go over the main ideas and results. Much of the material may be familiar so please feel free to skim briefly through the results or skip entirely. There is a lot of theory to cover so the proofs will only be sketched. For more details see for example (Bachman and Narici 2000).

5 A brief recap of Hilbert spaces

Firstly what is a Hilbert space? To answer that question let’s start with what kind of spaces we want to model.

Example 1 The Euclidean space together with the inner product

Example 2 The complex Euclidean space together with the inner product

You might be wondering why we took the conjugate in the definition of the inner product above. That is simply because we want the inner product to define a norm by

In the cases above note that this is the standard Euclidean norm

A Hilbert space is just this construction generalized to infinite dimensions. Firstly, the inner product is generalized as follows.

Definition 1 Given a vector space over where or , an inner product is a mapping

Such that the following hold for all and all

  1. Linearity: .

  2. Conjugate symmetry .

  3. Positivity if and only if .

In what follows we will take always take the base field to be , that is, we will consider real and complex vector spaces as the theory is the same. In the real case, the conjugate function just being the identity. Note that the first two properties also imply “antilinearity” of the second component as

A vector space with an inner product is called an inner product space. This inner product gives us a concept of geometry.

Definition 2 Given an inner product space we define

Furthermore, we say that are orthogonal if .

With this definition, Pythagoras’s theorem follows directly, as if are orthogonal then by the linearity of the inner product.

An important property of the inner product is the following

Proposition 1 (Cauchy Schwartz) Let be an inner product space, then it holds that

In particular, this implies that the inner product is continuous jointly, and in each component. An inner product space keeps the key properties of Euclidean space, namely the vector space structure and inner product. The third key property is that of completeness.

Definition 3 A Hilbert space is a complete inner product space . Where completeness is respect to the inner product norm

Cool! Now we know what is a Hilbert space. Some examples that have come up in previous posts are the space of square-integrable functions valued in another Hilbert space with the inner product

Another are the Sobolev spaces with the inner product

(I apologize for using the same notation for the Japanese bracket and the inner product). One result that carries over from Euclidean spaces to Hilbert spaces is the existence of projections.

Theorem 1 (Projection theorem) Let be a closed convex subset of a Hilbert space . Then, given there exists a unique such that

Furthermore, if is a (vector) subspace, then it holds that is the unique element in such that is orthogonal to . That is is the only one verifying

Proof. The idea of the proof is to consider a sequence that approaches the minimum distance

The inner product comes in to show that is a Cauchy sequence. Since is complete it converges to some . Then our assumptions on come in. Firstly, since is closed shows . Secondly, since is convex one can get uniqueness. The uniqueness is once more a consequence of the properties of the inner product.

A consequence of the above theorem is that orthogonal complements in Hilbert spaces exist.

Theorem 2 (Orthogonal complement) Let be a closed subspace of a vector space . Then we have that

Is a bijective isomorphism where we consider on the inner product

Proof. Given we know that there exists a unique closest point to (the projection of onto ). Furthermore, so we get the decomposition

This proves bijectivity, whereas the isomorphism property follows quickly from the orthogonality of .

An important corollary of this is the following

Corollary 1 Let be a vector space, then

Note that the imposition that is closed in Theorem 2 is necessary. Finite-dimensional subspaces are always closed, but infinite-dimensional subspaces may not be. For example, consider for some bounded and take to polynomials on . By the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stone

Another property of Euclidean space that Hilbert spaces reproduce is that of the existence of an orthonormal basis

Definition 4 Let be a Hilbert space and some index set. We say that is an orthonormal basis of if

And for every element there exist such that

Note that we impose no conditions on the Index set which may be countable or uncountable.

Definition 5 Given an index set and a normed vector space we say that is absolutely summable to and write

if given there exists some finite subset such that for every containing it holds that

If is countable, the definition says that converges to regardless of the order in which we sum. In fact, the following shows that, even if we start with an uncountable sequence we will always end up back in this case.

Proposition 2 Let be a normed space and be absolutely summable to , then only a countable number of the terms are non-zero. Let us take the nonzero terms and relabel them , then

Proof. Take such that

Then is countable (it is a countable union of countable sets) and a small reasoning shows that are the non-zero terms of . The fact that the nonzero sum to is just a consequence of the definition of absolute summability.

Now that we made sense of the sum over a potentially uncountable number of basis elements in (1), it remains to address the question of the existence of orthonormal basis.

Theorem 3 (Existence of orthonormal basis) Every Hilbert space has an orthonormal basis .

Proof. The proof is formally identical to the proof that every vector space has a basis space . Let be the collection of all orthonormal subsets of . That is, is comprised of sets of the form

Such that are orthonormal. Given an ordered chain we have the bound

As a result by <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zorn

The next result is the natural generalization of Pythagoras’s theorem to Hilbert spaces.

Theorem 4 (Parseval) Let be a Hilbert space with orthonormal basis . Then for every it holds that

Proof. We have that by the orthonormality of and the continuity of the inner product

As a result, is orthogonal to the closure of the span of , which by assumption is . By Corollary Corollary 1 we conclude that

This proves the first part of the theorem. The second follows by the first together with the orthonormality of .

The above shows that, on fixing a basis, every Hilbert space can be identified with a space of square-integrable sequences by the bijective isometry

In particular, every Hilbert space with a countable basis is isometric to . However, the identification is not “canonical” as it depends on the bases chosen. The next example is all pervasive (in fact it has even invaded our blog)

Example 3 (Plancherel) The Hilbert space of square integrable complex valued periodic functions has orthonormal basis

Thus, every function can be written as

Where is known as the Fourier transform of and defined by

Another interesting property of Euclidean space is that every element of the dual is represented by a vector in the space, that is

Here one can calculate directly that is the conjugate of the “matrix” defined by as a linear function

Where is the standard orthonormal basis of . In Hilbert spaces, the same result holds for Hilbert spaces

Theorem 5 (Riesz representation) Let be a Hilbert space, then given there exists a unique such that

Furthermore, .

Proof. Consider an orthonormal basis for . Then, just as in Euclidean space we have that

The fact that verifies (2) is a direct application of the (anti)-linearity and continuity of the inner product. Uniqueness following from the fact that if both verify the equality then for all

This can only occur if (hint take ). To verify the norm we can use that, for all with norm

The equality shows that whereas the inequality shows the converse , proving the theorem.

The previous theorem says that we have an antilinear isometry

This allows us to identify with . The identification is canonical as it does not depend on the bases chosen. Yes, we fixed a basis to prove it but the vector is unique independently of the basis. This allows us to make into a Hilbert space in a canonical way

Proposition 3 Let be a Hilbert space, then is also a Hilbert space, with inner product given by

Where we had to “swap the order” of the representatives of due to the anti-linearity of the mapping . Since is also a Hilbert space we can apply Riesz’s theorem to to show that is also a Hilbert space and there exists a canonical antilinear isometry

By construction, of it holds that

That is, is identified canonically with and, the identification is such that

In other words.

Theorem 6 Every Hilbert space is reflexive.

Consider now a Hilbert space and a linear operator . Then, for each we can define the linear form

As a result, there exists a unique representative of in which, to track the dependence on , we denote by . That is, verifies

A small reasoning shows that is a linear function of , that is, there exists with , or in other words

Definition 6 Given we denote by the unique element verifying (3) . If we say that is self adjoint.

If we think in terms of complex numbers, the adjoint of an element is and is self adjoint if and only if it is real. If now we consider the case where is and is given by some matrix then . As in these finite dimensional cases, the following proof is not difficult.

Proposition 4 Let with a Hilbert space. Then with .

Hilbert spaces provide us a way to guarantee existence and uniqueness to a wide class of problems, an important tool is Lax-Milgram’s theorem. We first need two definitions

Definition 7 We say that a mapping

on a vector space , is sesquilinear if is linear in the first component and antilinear in the second. That is, for all and :

Definition 8 Let be a normed vector space then we say that a sesquilinear form is coercive if it is continuous and there exists a constant such that

The coercivity condition essentially imposes that the bilinear form is not degenerate. As a particular example, a symmetric sesquilinear form is an inner product.

Theorem 7 (Lax Milgram) Let be respectively an coercive sesquilinear form and a linear form on a Hilbert space . Then there exists an invertible linear operator and such that

As a result, equation

has a unique solution . Furthermore, the solution operator is continuous with

Proof. For each fixed , we have that . As a result by Riesz’s representation theorem (Theorem 5) there exists a unique such that

Furthermore, it can be simply verified that the mapping is linear in . That is, there exists such that

The existence of the representative of is once more by Riesz’s representation theorem. We now show that verifies the desired properties. Firstly is continuous as, given

So dividing by on either side shows that . Now, is injective as if then

We now prove surjectivity of . Consider , then it holds that

As a result, . We deduce from the corollary of the orthogonal complement (Corollary 1) that . Thus, if we show that is closed invertibility follows. The estimate in (7) together with Cauchy Schwartz show that for all

As if is a Cauchy sequence then so must be . By completeness of , the sequence converges to some and we deduce, by continuity of , that . In consequence, is invertible, finally to show the bound on let us write then

Dividing on either side by concludes the proof.

If we had assumed that were anti-symmetric then the proof would have been simplified as would define an inner product . Applying Riesz’s theorem to this inner product (as opposed to the original one) would transform our equation (4) into

That is, to solve (4) it would suffice to take . In the case where is symmetric and real, we can also find by solving a minimization problem

Proposition 5 (Minimization formulation) Let be a symmetric coercive bilinear operator on a real Hilbert space . Then, problem (4) is equivalent to minimizing

Proof. To prove that a solution to (4) minimizes we can develop and simplify it using to obtain

To prove that a minimum of solves one can show by taking limits as in the expression

That for all

Taking and where is any shows and , which concludes the proof.

6 A little bit of operator theory

Finally, we wrap up with some operator theory. This will be revisited in a more detailed blog post on spectral theory. For now, we give the essentials.

Definition 9 We say that a linear operator where are two metric spaces is compact if is relatively compact for all bounded .

The above will be abbreviated . Note that, since every compact set is bounded must be bounded and thus continuous. That is

In practice, the following equivalent characterizations are useful.

Proposition 6 Let be two metric spaces, then the following are equivalent

  1. .

  2. is relatively compact where is the unit ball in .

  3. From every sequence where one can extract a subsequence converging in .

Proof. The prove the first two points are equivalent we observe that every bounded set is contained in for big enough. A general fact from topology is that closed subsets of compact sets are compact. This is enough to conclude the equivalence.

To prove that the last two points are equivalent we recall that in metric spaces compact is equvalent to sequentially compact.

An important property of compact operators is that they are preserved by continuous ones.

Proposition 7 Let . Where are metric spaces , then and are compact

Proof. This follows by the last equivalent characterization in Proposition 6.

We already saw that the space of compact operators is a subset of the space of linear operators. The structure of as a subspace is as follows

Proposition 8 Let be metric spaces then is a vector space. Furthermore, if is completee then is closed in . That is, if are compact and then is compact.

Proof. The first part again follows fromb the last equivalent characterization in Proposition 6. The second part relies on the fact that in complete spaces compact and <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Totally_bounded_space#:~:text=not

The reason we are interested in compact operators is that they are particularly simple. Note that every finite-dimensional operator (that is operators whose image is finite-dimensional) is compact by the Heine-Borel theorem. In fact, a good way of thinking of compact operators is to see them as finite-dimensional operators . Or more precisely, as the limit of them

Proposition 9 Let where is a separable Hilbert space . Then there exists a sequence of finite-dimensional operators such that

Proof. Since is separable there exists a countable orthonormal basis . If we denote for the projection of onto the space generated by then, by Parseval’s Theorem pointwise convergence holds

Consider the unit ball in . Then is relatively compact. So is totally bounded and given we can form a finite net of . By pointwise convergence, we can now take large enough so that for all

Now for any we can find such that . Using the triangle inequality

This concludes the proof.

For us, an important example of compact operators will be the solution operator of a PDE. This is because of the following theorem

Theorem 8 (Rellich-Kondrachov) Let be a bounded open domain in with smooth boundary. Then, given it holds that the natural inclusion

is compact for all .

For the proof of a more general version see (Taylor 2013) page 334. It is important to observe the restriction that is bounded is necessary and the theorem no longer holds if is replaced by .

Corollary 2 (Adding differentiability is compact) Let be a bounded open domain in with smooth boundary and , then every continuous operator

is compact.

Proof. We have that so we conclude by Rellich-Kondrachov’s Theorem 8 and the preservation of compact operators by continuous ones (Proposition 7).

Compact operators also have a nice spectral theory. Where we recall that the spectrum of an operator is defined as

Firstly, the spectrum of a compact operator is equal to it’s eigenvalues. Secondly the following holds

Theorem 9 (Spectral theorem) Let be compact and self adjoint on a Hilbert space . Then diagonalizes in an orthonormal basis. That is, there exists an orthonormal basis and such that

A more general result is possible but requires more theory so we reserve it for another day. That said, this type of discrete representation of is very useful and links up with the theory of trace class and Hilbert-Schmidt operators which we will discuss more in the future.

To end it all off we state without proof a theorem that will be useful in proving properties about the solution space to the solution of PDEs

Theorem 10 (Fredholm alternative) Let be a Hilbert space and . Consider then it holds that

Furthermore, it holds that

  1. is finite dimensional.

  2. is closed.

  3. .

We delay the proof till another day, in the meantime, see (Evans 2022) page 725.

7 References

Bachman, George, and Lawrence Narici. 2000. Functional Analysis. Courier Corporation. https://books.google.co.uk/books?hl=en&lr=&id=wCHtLumoGY4C&oi=fnd&pg=PR3&dq=narici+bachman&ots=vxU8cRul6s&sig=tGyszvXv-1psk-qyJ8ruM6S7l5I&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q=narici%20bachman&f=false.
Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.
Taylor, Michael. 2013. Partial Differential Equations II: Qualitative Studies of Linear Equations. Vol. 116. Springer Science & Business Media.

item.title: Study of densities part 1
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • A Malliavin differentiable random variable is supported in intervals.

  • If is almost nowhere zero, then has a density .

  • If is smooth then is smooth.

2 Why should I care?

The density of a random variable (if it exists) gives us complete information about it and is the fundamental goal of statistical inference.

3 Notation

Same as other posts, we recall in particular that is the space of Malliavin smooth random variables. Additionally, to simplify many expressions, we introduce the notation

4 Introduction

Our goal is to establish some fundamental properties on the existence of densities using Malliavin calculus. This is one of the first reasons why Malliavin calculus was conceived. Firstly, we recall that given a random variable valued in some metric space , the support of is defined to be the set of points such that

That is, has a positive probability of falling in any ball that contains . Our first result characterizes the support of real-valued Malliavin differentiable random variables.

Proposition 1 Let , then the support of is an interval.

Proof. Suppose not, then there must exist where are in the support of and isn’t. In particular, there exists such that

Where we also take small enough so that . Now take a smooth and bounded function with for and with for . By (1) it holds that (almost everywhere) . Combining proposition 5 and corollary 1 of the previous post, we obtain that is either or almost everywhere. That is,

But this contradicts that both are in the support of . This concludes the proof.

Ok, now we know the support of Malliavin differentiable functions are nicely shaped intervals. But what about densities, when can we guarantee their existence? Consider for example the simplest of all cases, . That is always takes the value . Then is Malliavin differentiable, however, it admits no density (no, the function does not count as a density). Let’s consider a case that is a bit less trivial, set Then, we have that is a Gaussian random variable with As a result, we obtain that will fail to have a density if (and only if) . Notice that, in the previously considered case where failed to have a density, we also had that became . The next proposition shows that this is not a fluke.

Theorem 1 (Existence of densities) Let . If almost everywhere, then has a density.

Proof. By the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radon

Then, by construction , so also . Suppose now that were continuous. Then we would have that, by the fundamental theorem of calculus . So by the chain rule

By taking norms on each side, we would deduce that, since is non-zero, must be zero. That is, , as desired. Of course, in general, will not be differentiable (unless is actually everywhere). So to complete the proof, all that remains is to approximate by continuous functions and take limits. See (Nualart and Nualart 2018) page 120 for the details.

The question is, can we give an explicit formula for the density of . The answer is (under some stricter conditions) yes, and the proof uses a similar trick as our last one.

Theorem 2 (Density expression) Let such that is non-zero almost everywhere. Suppose further that . Then has a density given by

Proof. Let us consider and set as before

Then by the chain rule and the fundamental theorem of calculus, we have that

By taking expectations on both sides, and using that the Skorokhod integral is the adjoint of the Malliavin derivative, and Fubini we obtain

Since was any smooth function this concludes the proof (as we can always approximate indicator functions by smooth functions).

To show the differentiability of we need the following lemma which will let us transfer- derivatives onto the density .

Lemma 1 (Integration by parts) Let be such that almost everywhere. Then, given any and , there exists such that

Proof. We proceed by induction and consider first the case . We want to integrate parts in a probabilistic integral, as opposed to a classical deterministic one, against the Lebesgue measure. The idea is therefore to find a way to introduce the Malliavin differential and then use the Skorokhod integral to move derivates. To do so, we use one of our previous tricks to write

By now taking expectations, moving into the inner product on , and using that Skorokhod integral is the adjoint of the Malliavin derivative, we get that

Where because the Skorokhod integral maps into . This resembles the fact that the derivative maps the Schwartz space to itself. A proof of this can be found in Hairer’s online notes (Hairer 2021) page 15. Now suppose that the lemma holds for , then by the same procedure,

By the previous comment, since also , which concludes the proof.

We now finally show that, if can be differentiated multiple times, then so can its density.

Theorem 3 (Smooth variables have smooth densities) Let such that almost everywhere, then has a smooth density with respect to the Lebesgue measure.

Proof. By Theorem 2 we know that has a density . To show that is smooth, by Sobolev embeddings it is sufficient to show that is weakly differentiable with order for all . That is, for all , there exists a function such that

We apply the integration by parts lemma to shift derivatives from to the weight :

Using the fundamental theorem of calculus, we write . Substituting this into the expectation and applying Fubini’s theorem yields:

Repeating this process inductively demonstrates that the -th weak derivative of the density is explicitly given by:

Since this bounded expectation exists for all , is smooth.

Finally, we comment that all these theorems carry over to the case where is a vector-valued random variable (as opposed to real-valued). In this case, the condition for the existence of densities becomes that the matrix of derivatives has non-zero determinant almost everywhere. We refer the reader to (Nualart and Nualart 2018) Chapter or (Hairer 2021) chapter for the multidimensional theory.

5 References

Hairer, M. 2021. “Introduction to Malliavin Calculus.” https://www.hairer.org/notes/Malliavin.pdf.
Nualart, David, and Eulalia Nualart. 2018. Introduction to Malliavin Calculus. Vol. 9. Cambridge University Press. https://books.google.co.uk/books?hl=zh-CN&lr=&id=l_1uDwAAQBAJ&oi=fnd&pg=PR11&dq=nualart+introduction+malliavin&ots=_JuMhMkTMt&sig=Tx5y00u4kMNs73jLtMEs-kyXAuU&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q=nualart\%20introduction\%20malliavin&f=false.

item.title: PDEs 1: The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.link: https://nowheredifferentiable.com/posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/
item.description:
Three line summary

  • The Fourier transform defines a linear isometry on the space of square-integrable complex-valued functions and has as inverse the inverse Fourier transform. This allows us to write these functions as a superposition of harmonic functions.

  • Linear differential operators act as a simple polynomial multiplication on the frequency domain. Smooth functions have Fourier transforms that decay quickly and vice-versa. This correspondence also allows us to introduce fractional differential operators and pseudo differential operators such as .

  • The Sobolev spaces and tempered distributions are complete spaces of functions to which we can extend differential operators.

Introduction

Hi everyone, welcome to the first post in a new series focusing on partial differential equations (PDEs). In this post, we’ll introduce some of the key analytical tools used to solve them. These tools will play a prominent role in the future posts of this series and in other series to come. We’ll begin by introducing some notation and necessary preliminaries on integration. Next, we will introduce the Fourier transform, which is the fundamental object of harmonic analysis and plays a fundamental role in partial differential equations (PDEs), probability theory, and number theory. In particular, we will show in future posts how many linear PDEs such as the heat equation can be solved using it.

After this, we’ll discuss the generalization of what it means to solve a PDE. To do so we will need to introduce the space of (tempered) distributions and Sobolev spaces . These two spaces will be crucial in allowing us to extend the concept of solution to PDE to these spaces and use their completeness to prove the existence, uniqueness, and regularity of solutions.

Much of the material here contained can be found in my <https://gredos.usal.es/bitstream/handle/10366/145629/TFG

Notation

We write and to denote the space of equivalence classes of integrable functions from to and respectively. Given a function defined on a factor space we shall use to stand for the slightly more cumbersome . Given we shall write as is standard

and whenever the expression appears we will assume implicitly that . Given we will write to denote its norm and the Japanese bracket will signify

Finally, we will employ the notation to mean that there exists some constant such that . If the value of depends on some other parameter such as the dimension we shall make this explicit by writing .

Some useful results on integration

In PDEs, it is essential to be able to commute limits and differentials with both summation and integration. The main way of doing this is the dominated and monotone convergence theorem together with the following two propositions which we state in full generality for functions valued in a Banach space . Though for now, we will only need the case . On first reading, this can be assumed for commodity.

Proposition 1 (Continuity of the integral) Let be a measure space, a first countable metric space, a Banach space and

such that is (Bochner) integrable for all and such that

Then given we have that

In consequence, if is continuous so is .

Proof. The proof is an application of the dominated convergence theorem to the sequence of functions where is some sequence converging to and the fact that, in first countable spaces, sequential continuity is equivalent to continuity.

Proposition 2 ({Differentiation under the integral sign}) Let be a measure space, be a convex open interval of , and a Banach space. Consider

such that:

  1. is measurable for every and integrable for some .

  2. For each we have that is differentiable and there exists an integrable function such that

    Then it holds that

Proof. First, we show that the integral on the left-hand side of the equation above makes sense. Let be any, then by the mean value inequality we have that

Thus, we may apply the previous proposition to commute the limit with the integral

Where in the first equality we also used the linearity of the Bochner integral and in the last equality we used that by hypothesis is everywhere differentiable. This concludes the proof.

In the literature the usual hypothesis is that is integrable everywhere (Aguirre and Ibáñez 2002, 108), however as we have seen it is sufficient to require the integrability at a point.

1 The Fourier transform on

These preliminaries out of the way, we now get to our first main section. We assume the reader has encountered the Fourier transform before, and limit ourselves to giving a rundown of the theory and main ideas, taking the reader through a basic definition to an extension of it. To make the Fourier transform an isometry we will use the following convention.

Definition 1 Given the Fourier transform of and the inverse Fourier transform of are defined respectively by

Other exponents such as can also be used with identical results modulo constants. Let us introduce the notation for the Fourier transform (on ) and its inverse. The following proposition holds.

Proposition 3 (Properties of ) Given

  1. The Fourier transform is a continuous linear operators .

  2. If then:

  3. If is absolutely continuous in for almost every then then

Proof. The proof of the first point is an application of the triangle inequality as

and similarly for the inverse. The second point follows by differentiation under the integral Proposition 2. The third point can be proved by using integration by parts and the observation that every absolutely continuous function must go to zero at infinity.

We note that the analogous holds for the inverse Fourier transform where it is only necessary to remove the minus sign in and a change of the sign in . The proof can be completed in an identical fashion or by noting that .

The importance of these last two properties is that they give us information on the Fourier transform of a function based on its regularity and decay. They can be stated informally as decay gives regularity and regularity gives decay (as the Fourier transform of any decays to 0 at infinity by the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann In general, the Fourier transform of an integrable function is not itself integrable. That is, is not closed under the Fourier transform. We now introduce a space that is closed under the Fourier transform; the Schwartz space, which can be thought of as the space of infinitely regular functions with infinite decay:

Definition 2 The Schwartz space is:

By applying Proposition 3 we deduce that, given

Hence, the Fourier transform and the inverse Fourier transform restrict to endomorphisms of the Schwartz space which we will denote respectively by

The next item on the agenda is Plancherel’s theorem which is proven via the following lemma, in which we shall use the notation

for the inner product on .

Lemma 1 (Plancherel for Schwartz functions) Given

The first point can be proved by a direct application of Fubini. The second point is trickier and is the crux of why we call the inverse Fourier transform. The proof can be found in (Taylor 1996) pages 222-226. From Lemma 1 and Lemma 1 we deduce immediately that, given a Schwartz function ,

That is, the restrictions of and

are linear unitary operators which are the inverse the one to the other. We obtain the following result.

Proposition 4 (Plancherel’s theorem) and may be extended to unitary operators:

With . \end {proposition}

Proof. This is a immediate result of the density of in the larger and the completeness of together with Lemma 1 and Plancherel for Lemma 1. As, by a simple limiting argument, it is easy to see that continuous extensions of continuous linear operators preserve the norm and the inverses of the operators in question.

It is reasonable to wonder if this extended Fourier transform coincides with our initial definition when reasonable. That is, whether given a function we have that

This indeed holds as can be seen by taking a sequence of functions converging to in and in . As then converges uniformly to by Proposition 3 (The) and in to by Plancherel Proposition 4, from where we deduce that as desired .

2 The Fourier transform of periodic functions

We now extend our previous results to periodic functions, though, for any period, analogous results may be achieved. By identifying periodic functions with functions on the torus we will work with functions As we will see strikingly similar results are achieved in this setting

Definition 3 Given we define the -th Fourier coefficient of as:

We thus obtain a function on which we shall call the Fourier series of and a continuous linear function which we shall denote as in the euclidean case:

where is the set of bounded sequences from to . As before we have the following result: \begin{proposition} Given if is absolutely continuous in for almost every then

In particular if we have that:

and as a result we have that is of rapid decrease (i.e.  decreases faster than the inverse of any polynomial). We thus have, as before, an induced map:

Where are the sequences from to that are of rapid decrease (this space now plays the role of the Schwartz space ).

Similarly to the non-periodic case we now define

with:

This is indeed smooth as, by the rapid decay of , we may differentiate under the integral sign Proposition 2 (we recall that sums are just integrals against discrete measures) to commute all derivatives with the above sum to obtain that

It is now possible to prove as with the euclidean case that

and that analogously given smooth, and of rapid decrease

See for example (Taylor 1996) pages 197-206. We conclude that are unitary linear functions and that hence:

Proposition 5 (Plancherel’s (periodic) theorem) and may be extended to unitary operators:

with

We note that, as for the Euclidean case, given by an identical argument it holds that

and where now Plancherel’s theorem gives that, for such :

3 Distributions and Sobolev Spaces

Here we will quickly recall the concepts of tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces, which are concepts of utmost importance in the field of PDEs and Fourier analysis. The idea is as follows, given a topological vector space we denote the dual of by

Furthermore, given and we use the notation

The reason for this is that can often be interpreted as the inner product of against in a suitable Hilbert space. In our case, this Hilbert space will be and the above interpretation will allow us to extend differentiation and the Fourier transform by a technique called “duality”. We will apply this where , first we need to introduce a topology on . In general, given a real vector space together with a countable family of semi-norms with the property that: given , there exists such that . Then

is a translation invariant metric on . In the case of the Schwartz space we give it the topology induced by

Though other families of semi-norms the reader may be familiar with such as

induce equivalent topologies. We note that with this metric is complete.

For a quick proof based on the fundamental theorem of calculus see for example (Folland 1999) page 237.

Definition 4 The space of tempered distributions is the dual space to with the topology generated by . We write it .

One may verify that we have the inclusion

where given we define

Let us write for the transpose of a linear function and recall that the Fourier transform is an endomorphism of the Schwartz space. Then, given two Schwartz functions we have already seen that, by a simple application of Fubini,

and integration by parts gives

This gives us a way of extending the Fourier transform and differentiation to the space of tempered distributions.

Definition 5 Given and we define the (distributional) Fourier transform of by

and the (weak) ’th derivative of by

The method we used above to extend is called the duality method and appears very frequently. Another way of writing the above definition is:

Due to our previous discussion, we have that with this definition

Just as one would desire.

Two other operations that are permitted on are multiplication by functions of polynomial growth and the application of the inverse Fourier transform which we shall, as for functions, denote by . Both definitions are once again being given by duality.

Before ending our discussion of (scalar) tempered distributions we comment on some generalizations. We first note that the previous discussion works equivalently for vector-valued distributions, i.e. elements of the dual to , which we shall denote by

where the only change is that the inclusion of integrable functions is now given by

with defined by

In both cases we have that, by duality, due to the (3), given a tempered distribution and

As we have observed before multiplication by functions of polynomial growth is a well-defined operation on so the above expressions are also well defined. A quick verification also shows that, since Plancherel’s theorem holds for all Schwartz functions,

Finally, in addition to “changing the image” of our distributions, we may also “change the domain” by considering, for example, periodic tempered distributions. Where now, as we saw in the section on the Fourier transform, takes the place of the Schwartz space and where we place on the topology defined by the countable family of semi-norms:

and denote its dual by Note that, as the domain is bounded, multiplication by polynomials to ensure rapid decrease is redundant. By defining as is natural the Fourier series of a periodic distribution by the sequence (which can be shown to be of polynomial growth)

and its -th distributional derivative by

we derive formulas analogous to the ones seen in the section on the Fourier transform for “periodic” distributions as well. Namely:

To prove it all we have to do is apply Plancherel on and move terms around via duality,

This proves the first part and the second can be proved directly by considering the relevant definitions.

To sum up what we have seen, due to the natural inclusion of integrable functions in the space of tempered distributions and the analogous inclusion in the periodic case, the notion of Fourier transform and differentiation extends to the larger space of tempered distributions. This allows us to manipulate rough functions (periodic or non-periodic) as if they had Fourier transforms and were smooth. As we shall see, this will prove of great use when obtaining “distributional solutions” to some PDEs. We now give the general method by which this achieved

Definition 6 Consider a mapping

that extends to

where is some subset of containing . Then, given we say that a distributional solution to is any tempered distribution verifying .

In the above definition, typically defines a linear or non-linear differential equation. Note that the above definition may be extended without any difficulty to the case of (periodic) distributional solutions in the case where .

Example 1 Set , as we have seen previously extends to with

Solving formally yields . However, division by is ill-defined at the origin in . A unique solution only exists if we restrict our domain (e.g., requiring in suitable homogeneous spaces) to bypass the singularity.

4 Sobolev spaces

Sobolev spaces form a particular case of tempered distributions that we interpret as being smooth and integrable up to sufficient orders.

Definition 7 Given we define the Sobolev space as:

Where, we consider as a subspace of using (11).

We may interpret the Sobolev space as the space of times differentiable functions in and we give it the norm:

Note that it is not enough to require as, for example, the tempered distribution has a derivative equal to zero however is not itself in . Now, by Plancherel for Lemma 1, the Fourier transform is an automorphism of , by using Proposition 3 we deduce that

from which we deduce that

In fact, since the Fourier transform is a unitary transformation on , the same reasoning gives

From the two equations above we deduce that if we define for a given real number (including negative numbers!) the -th order Sobolev space as

and give it the norm

then our new definition is equivalent to the previous one (16) when is a positive integer. We have thus found how to generalize the concept of Sobolev space to all real orders and obtained a useful way of characterizing them and giving a neat expression for their norm. Nonetheless, it will always be useful to retain the first definition based on derivatives, as it carries with it the motivation behind the definition of Sobolev spaces. As was the case with tempered distributions we can extend the concept of Sobolev space to periodic domains by defining given an integer the Sobolev space as the space of square-integrable periodic functions with distributional derivatives themselves square integrable. Explicitly we define:

Using the same method as before, by the correspondence between regularity and decay for the Fourier transform of periodic functions Proposition 4 and Proposition 5, we deduce that

which leads us as in the previous case to define for the more general Sobolev space

and to give it the norm

where of course the definitions in (20) and (21) coincide for . Note that, by the previous discussion, we have that both in the euclidean and periodic case

One major advantage of working with the Sobolev spaces is that, differently to the classical space of smooth functions , they form a Hilbert space with the inner product given by

This gives one access to all the power of functional analysis and is invaluable in proofs. However, at the end of the day wishes to prove that solutions with smooth initial data are themselves smooth in a classical sense. This can be done by showing that the solution belongs to a Sobolev space of high enough order together with the following two results.

Lemma 2 (Sobolev Embedding into Continuous Functions) Given with . Then the Fourier series of is absolutely convergent and with the bound

Proof. The proof is an application of the Cauchy-Schwartz inequality. We have that

In consequence, the sum

converges absolutely (and uniformly). Since by Plancherel’s Theorem, the above sum also converges in to we deduce that (23) converges almost everywhere to (for example by taking a subsequence of the above sum that converges almost everywhere to ). Therefore

which is

The continuity of follows from the point-wise equality

together with the monotone convergence theorem applied to with the counting measure .

As a corollary of this, we have the following two results

Proposition 6 (Sobolev embedding) Let where . Then .

Proof. By (Lemma 2), we may apply the previous proposition to deduce that is continuous for all . Therefore it suffices to show that for the distributional derivatives are also the classical derivatives of which we denote by . By the hypothesis placed on , we have that the series

is absolutely convergent (by Lemma 2), and hence, we may commute the derivatives of with the sum in its Fourier series to deduce the point-wise equality

Now, note that by using (15) for the Fourier coefficients of distributions we also have that the equality

holds in . From these last two equalities, we deduce that almost everywhere which concludes our proof.

As before the previous results also have a euclidean analog whose proof is identical in replacing all of the above sums over with integrals over . Finally, we conclude this post with a neat little trick. Given smooth and some differential operator we have that

The term is called a Fourier multiplier and there is no need to limit ourselves to polynomials. In fact, we may make the general definition that for a function of two variables

This leads to the definition of pseudo-differential operators. A particular case is that of fractional operators. We now show an example.

Example 2 Given smooth we have that

As a result, we define the fractional Laplacian for all as

By contrast, the Bessel potential operator is defined using the Japanese bracket: .

This post is already getting a bit long (if you’ve stuck in till the end I salute you), so we leave it off here with a neat little exercise (our first of this blog)

Exercise 1 How should we define Once you do so show that for all , the operator defines a linear bijective isometry

In our future post we will discuss the well posedness of second order elliptic equations, and if we have time discuss the heat equation and how the Fourier transform affords provides us with an elegant solution.

5 References

Aguirre, José A Facenda, and Francisco José Freniche Ibáñez. 2002. Integración de Funciones de Varias Variables. Ediciones Pirámide. https://books.google.co.uk/books/about/Integraci%C3%B3n_de_funciones_de_varias_vari.html?id=uuHbOgAACAAJ&redir_esc=y.
Folland, Gerald B. 1999. Real Analysis: Modern Techniques and Their Applications. Vol. 40. John Wiley & Sons.
Taylor, Michael. 1996. Partial Differential Equations i: Basic Theory. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-1-4419-7055-8.
