item.title: MC 4: The Malliavin derivative, part 2
item.dc:creator: Billy Summers
item.description:
1 The Malliavin derivative as a Fr'{e

chet derivative} Let be the Banach space of all continuous functions such that. On this space, we can associate a special Borel probability measure such that is a Brownian motion. Given a random variable on , i.e. a -measurable function , we want to know how the value changes upon perturbing the path by a small quantity . This can be described by the Fr'{e}chet derivative , which is a bounded linear map , i.e. a member of the dual space, giving the best linear approximation to the difference . Formally, satisfies

If has a Fr'{e}chet derivative , we say it is Fr'{e}chet differentiable.

Within the Banach space lies a Hilbert space of distinguished elements. This is the space of paths of the form

for some . In other words, it is the space of functions on starting at 0. Its inner product is given by

is continuously imbedded in by the theory of Sobolev spaces. We call the Cameron-Martin space. It acts in some sense as the heart of with the probability measure , with its elements having better analytical properties compared to a general element in . Ideally, we could restrict to this space and only work here, but unfortunately is not a measure on (in particular, it is not -additive), forcing us to work in a larger Banach space.

Returning to our Fr'{e}chet differentiable random variable , given some path , we consider the restriction of its derivative at to , namely . Since is continously imbedded in , this restriction is an element of . Then, as is a Hilbert space, the dual space is isomorphic to through its inner product, so there exists some such that

This object is precisely the Malliavin derivative of .

2 The Skorokhod integral and the Malliavin derivative

Given a stochastic process such thatis-measurable for all, let

be its chaos expansion for some symmetric in the first variables. Recall we say is Skorokhod integrable, and define its Skorokhod integral by

whenever this sum converges in . The following result is fundamental.

Theorem 1 The Skorokhod integral and Malliavin derivative are adjoint in the following sense:

Let be a Skorokhod-integrable. Let be a Malliavin differentiable random variable. Then

More concretely,

Proof. As usual, we apply the definitions in terms of the chaos expansions. Let

be the chaos expansion of , and

the chaos expansion of . Then

and on the other side,

Finally, by definition of the symmetrization,

where we change variables , use the property that is symmetric, and apply Fubini’s theorem.

Remark 1. The symbol is often used for a divergence-like operator in Hodge theory. The analogy with our case is that in the Hodge situation, is defined via a duality formula which looks like , where is the exterior derivative on differential forms. Indeed, even in vector calculus, the negative of the divergence is in some sense adjoint to the gradient:

whenever has zero boundary. So, in a sense, the Skorokhod integral is just a divergence operator.

Using this, we can immediately prove the following:

Corollary 1 Let be a sequence of Skorokhod-integrable stochastic processes. Suppose there exist and such thatin, and in . Then is Skorokhod integrable, and .

Proof. Recall that Skorokhod integrability of can be expressed in terms of convergence of the series

where are the components of the chaos expansion of . Since strongly in , and each is Skorokohod integrable, the components of their chaos expansions must satisfy the above condition, and we can take limits.

Let . Then by adjointness,

Taking limits on both sides and using adjointness on the limiting objects gives us

Then, since is dense in , we see that a.s., as required.

Remark 2. Perhaps a more intuitive way to say the Skorokhod integral is “closable” in the book’s words is that it is sequentially continuous as a map with respect to the strong topology in its domain and weak topology in its codomain.

Theorem 2 Let be a Skorokhod integrable random process, and let be such that is also Skorokhod integrable. Then

almost surely.

Proof. Suppose has finite chaos expansion, and choose some also with finite chaos expansion. Then by adjointness and the product rule,

Since the set of all test functions with finite chaos expansion is dense in , we conclude the result for with finite chaos expansion. For general , we approximate.

Remark 3. A similar formula crops up in vector calculus, namely the following:

where is a scalar function and a vector field. Again, in the above theorem, the Malliavin derivative takes the place of the gradient, the Skorokhod integral take the place of the divergence, and the usual inner product on (the dot product) is replaced with the inner product. There is a sign difference owing to the fact the adjointness in the Malliavin case does not induce a sign change, unlike in the vector calculus case (see the remark above).

Theorem 3 Let be a stochastic process such that for all , is in , is Skorokhod integrable, and

Then lies in , and

Remark 4. The technical constraints in the theorem above are an unfortunate consequence of the fact the Skorokhod and Malliavin operators and are not defined on the full space and respectively - we have to ensure an operator throws us to the right spot before we can consider applying the other one.

Note that this theorem is simply an expression of the Malliavin derivative and Skorokhod integral’s failure to commute, with the error simply being the identity on . That is,

This contrasts with our vector calculus analogy, where the divergence and gradient most certainly commute (assuming enough regularity).

3 References

item.title: MC 3: The Malliavin Derivative 1
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • The Malliavin derivative is an operator defined by manipulating the chaos expansion of a square-integrable random variable.

  • The Malliavin derivative transforms square-integrable variables into square integrable processes.

  • The Malliavin derivative shares some properties with the classic derivative such as the product rule and chain rule, but the fundamental theorem of calculus only holds in some special cases.

2 Why should I care?

The Malliavin derivative is (somewhat unsurprisingly) a fundamental object of Malliavin calculus and has many applications in finance, numerical methods, and optimal control.

3 Notation

The same as in previous posts. Furthermore we will shorten the notation and to and respectively.

4 Introduction

The Malliavin derivative was originally introduced as an operator associated with the Fréchet differential of random variables . The aforementioned construction provides some motivation behind the Malliavin derivative and will be developed in the next post. However, a more general construction can be obtained via the chaos expansion.

Definition 1 Given we say that is Malliavin differentiable if

and denote the space of Malliavin differentiable functions by

Furthermore, we define the Malliavin derivative of as

The first questions to be asked is: “what kind of object is the Malliavin derivative of a random variable? What is the link between and ? Is even a norm?”. We answer this in the next proposition and corollary.

Proposition 1 The Malliavin derivative is a bounded linear operator with

Proof. The proof of the first part of the proposition is a straightforward application of Itô’s -th isometry and the monotone convergence theorem as we have that

This establishes boundedness and the equality of the proposition. Finally, the linearity of follows from the linearity of the iterated Itô integrals (which itself is a consequence of the linearity of the Itô integral).

In summary, the Malliavin derivative turns a square-integrable random variable into a possibly non-adapted, stochastic process. You may recall from our previous posts that we had an operator that went in the opposite direction. The Skorokhod integral . In fact, the Malliavin derivative and the Skorokhod integral are adjoint operators in a sense that will be made precise in the next post. For now, we show that, as occurs with the ordinary derivative, a random variable has Malliavin derivative if and only if it is constant.

Corollary 1 is an inner product space. Furthermore

Proof. The inner product properties hold as is induced by the inner product of the graph space . This shows that is am inner product. The second part follows from the fact that

So if and only if for all , which in turn is equivalent to . Where we recall that by convention and was defined as the identity on . This concludes the proof.

Before moving on we show a motivating example. Let us consider some deterministic function and set Then, by construction, we have so

This is a nice result and it might suggest something akin to the fundamental theorem of calculus such as for any Skorokhod integrable process . However, this will not hold in general and as, will be seen in the next post, occurs if and only if is a deterministic function in .\\ This said, we now show that is closed in the sense that: given a convergent sequence . If the derivatives converge then also .

Proposition 2 The space is a Hilbert space.

Proof. It only remains to show that is complete. Let be Cauchy. Or equivalently, by Corollary 1, is Cauchy in and in .

Since is complete must converge to some . Let us write the respective chaos expansions as

By Itô’s -th isometry and the convergence we deduce that also for each . By now applying Fatou’s lemma and the fact that is by hypothesis Cauchy in we obtain that

As desired.

We now conclude this post by stating two properties of the Malliavin derivative that are analogous to those verified by the derivative of ordinary functions. Firstly, an analog to the chain rule for the ordinary derivative. The proof can be found on page of Nunno and Øksendal’s book (Nunno, Øksendal, and Proske 2008) but is rather technical and relies on Hermite polynomials which were not discussed previously, so we omit it.

Definition 2 We write for the space of square integrable random variables such that for all but finitely many .

Proposition 3 (Product rule) Given it holds that

Finally, though we shall not use it, we mention that if is the dual of the Schwartz space and we construct a probability measure called the white noise probability measure then the following version of the chain rule also holds (see (Nunno, Øksendal, and Proske 2008) page ).

Proposition 4 (Chain rule) Consider with and such that for each . Then it holds that with

5 Extending past p=2

The Malliavin derivative lets us define a derivative on a subset of . However, it may also be useful to have a concept of derivative on random variables in . We now explain how to do this via an alternative construction of the Malliavin derivative. First of all, consider the set of cylindrical variables

That is, is the set of all smooth functions with bounded derivatives of Wiener integrals of deterministic functions. Let us use the abbreviation . Then, the results in the previous section show that the Malliavin differential of a cylindrical variable is

One can also start directly with the above equation as the defintion of Malliavin differential. In this case it is not clear that is well defined (that is, independent of the representation of ). However it is, see (Hairer 2021) page 10. Analogously to how one defines the norm on Sobolev spaces, we now take any and define a norm on by

Then, is a continuous linear operator on to . As a result, may be extended to the closure of (this relies on Meyer inequalities show that the Malliavin derivative is closable in for ). We denote this closure by and by abuse of notation also write for the continuous extension of to . Note that by definition of the norm , necessarily is a subset of . In this way, we have been able to extend the Malliavin differential to . Explicitly, we have that

Where is a sequence converging to in . Furthermore, we note that by the previous discussion coincides with our previous definition of the Malliavin differential when . For the case we define

We now conclude with an extension of the chain rule which can be used even when does not have bounded derivative.

Proposition 5 (Chain rule for ) Let and consider such that for some . Then , where . Furthermore,

Proof. By the mean value inequality we have that

As a result, since , we have that

Furthermore, the growth condition implies . Since by definition, we apply the generalized Hölder inequality with exponents and . Observing that , we obtain

This establishes that .

We now take a sequence of cylindrical random variables converging to in and an approximation to the identity . Let us set .

The final equality holds due to the identical bound established in (2) and the convergence of to respectively.

Essentially the previous proposition says that, if is differentiable and the derivative of doesn’t grow to fast (depending on the integrability of ), then is also differentiable and we can apply the chain rule. The integrability of depending on the integrability of and the growth of .

5.1 Example application

For example, in the case we could take to deduce that

If is an indicator function for some we obtain that

From here we deduce that . As we have seen, this occurs if and only if is constant, so necessarily or . Identifying sets with functions, we have just proved the following

Corollary 2 Given we have that if and only if (almost everywhere) or .

5.2 Multiple derivatives

Finally we comment on how it is possible to iterate the Malliavin derivative. Given a cylindrical process we should have that, with Einstein notation

As a result, we define given

In the same fashion as before, we can now define the -th differential norm as

Where we use the convention . Then we simply define to be the completion of with this norm. Finally, we define

6 References

Hairer, M. 2021. “Introduction to Malliavin Calculus.” https://www.hairer.org/notes/Malliavin.pdf.
Nunno, Giulia Di, Bernt Øksendal, and Frank Proske. 2008. Malliavin Calculus for lévy Processes with Applications to Finance. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-540-78572-9.

item.title: MC 2: The Skorokhod Integral
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • By fixing , one can obtain a chaos expansion for (possibly non-adapted) square integrable stochastic processes .

  • The Itô integral of an Itô integrable process has a chaos expansion.

  • This chaos expansion can converge even when is not adapted to the filtration (and thus not Itô integrable). This allows us to extend the Itô integral to non-adapted processes.

2 Why should I care?

If you want to define the Malliavin derivative you need the Skorokhod integral.

3 Notation

The same as in the previous post on the chaos expansion. We will also write for the space of Itô integrable functions.

4 The chaos expansion of an Itô integral

Our goal in this post is to construct the Skorokhod integral. This serves as a generalization of the Itô integral and the starting point for the definition of the Malliavin derivative. How is this done? Let us first consider a (not-necessarily adapted) stochastic process such that for each . Then we know that by the chaos expansion proved in the previous post, for each there exists such that

Let us write . Note that we are now considering as a function of variables instead of . In particular, we will be able to consider expressions like later on. The first thing we do is study what the adaptedness of means in term of the functions appearing in its chaos expansion.

Lemma 1 Let for each , then is adapted iff

Proof. Firstly, we note that a stochastic process is adapted iff Since the Itô integral is a martingale, we obtain that, by commuting the sum and using the uniqueness of the chaos expansion this is equivalent to requiring that, for all where the commutation of the sum and the integral is justified by the convergence of the chaos expansion ( convergence would have been enough).

In particular, we obtain that, since is already symmetric in its first n-coordinates, its symmetrization verifies that

Using this relationship we can directly calculate the Itô integral of a stochastic process to obtain that.

Theorem 1 Let then the Itô integral of is

Proof. This is a direct calculation using the previous result as

5 The Skorokhod integral

The last term appearing in the equality is what we will call the Skorokhod integral.

Definition 1 Let be a stochastic process such that

Then we will say that has Skorokhod integral and write .

As we saw in the previous theorem the Skorokhod integral is equal to the Itô integral for all stochastic processes in . However, it may also be defined for non-adapted stochastic processes. In fact, by using the orthogonality of the iterated integrals (what we called Itô’s -th isometry in the last post [@Chaosblog], we deduce the following).

Proposition 1 A stochastic process has a Skorokhod integral iff

Proof. By Itô’s -th isometry we have that

Of course, a priori the above condition is not that easy to check for a given function as it involves calculating the chaos expansion for the given process . In some cases however it is possible, to consider for example the stochastic process defined by on the interval . Then we have that

Thus, for all we have that

So with

Note however that the Itô integral of is undefined as it is not adapted. Since the Skorokhod integral of is equal to , the above example shows how one cannot simply “pull out constants in ” in the sense that, if is a random variable independent of and , then

Though this may seem unintuitive, it is a consequence of the fact that, even though may not depend on , the terms

can depend on . Despite this, the Skorokhod integral still maintains some of the natural properties we associate with integration.

Proposition 2 Let ,. Then it holds that

  • with.

  • .

  • for any measurable subset. Furthermore, if then

Proof. The first property is a consequence of the chaos expansion’s linearity (which is itself a consequence of the linearity of iterated Itô integration). The second is due to the expectation of the Itô integral being . The final property is a consequence of the fact that the chaos expansion of is

Which shown by the equivalent characterization of Skorokhod functions that . The final property is a consequence of the previously proved linearity.

We now know what the Skorokhod expansion is, how to characterize it, and its main properties, in the next post we will construct the Malliavin derivative as its adjoint.

6 References

item.title: The Bochner Integral
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
This is the first post in a series on extending analysis from real-valued functions to functions valued in Banach spaces. Our first topic is the Bochner integral. That is, the extension of the Lebesgue integral to functions valued in Banach spaces.

1 Three line summary

  • The Bochner integral is a way of integrating functions from a measure space to a Banach space.

  • Like the Lebesgue integral, it is first constructed for piecewise constant functions and extended continuously to the completion .

  • The completion can be explicitly described as the space of functions with separable image and with finite norm. This naturally leads to the definition of spaces.

2 Notation

  1. We consider a measure space which may not be -finite, and a Banach space where is the Borel sigma-algebra (that is, the smallest -algebra on containing all of the open sets) of .

  2. We denote the dual space of as and given we denote the pairing of and as .

  3. Given a subset we denote the indicator function of as .

  4. Given and we define the function by

3 Strong measurability

Our goal is to define integration for functions valued in a Banach space

When working with measure spaces, we are, in general, not interested in what happens on sets of measure . As a result, we are contented with properties of interest holding perhaps not everywhere but almost everywhere.

Definition 1 A property is said to hold -almost everywhere if there exists a set with such that the property holds on .

As anticipated, we first consider the class of simple functions

We can define their integral quite naturally as

If we take equivalence classes and identify functions that are equal almost everywhere, we can define the norm

A verification shows that integration is linear and for all .

That is, integration is a linear and continuous map

Since is a complete, we can linearly extend integration in a unique way to the completion of . The space , which can be built through taking limits of simple functions, is thus the space of functions that we can integrate. Our next step is to figure out what this is.

Definition 2 We say a function is -strongly measurable if there exists a sequence of simple functions such that

Since the simple are separately valued (that is, is separable), will also be (almost everywhere) separably valued. As a result we will always end up working with separable Banach spaces. The following properties are of use

Exercise 1 Let be a separable Banach space with dual , show that

  1. There exists such that

    Such a sequence is called a norming sequence.

  2. The Borel -algebra is equal to the -algebra generated by , and by . That is,

    If is not separable, the inclusion may fail. See (Vakhania, Tarieladze, and Chobanyan 2012) page 23 for a counterexample.

  3. A function is measurable if and only if it is weakly measurable. That is, if and only if for all the function is measurable.

  4. The dual with the weak- topology (the topology generated by viewed as a subset of ) is separable .

TipHint

  1. Consider a countable dense subset of . By the Hahn-Banach theorem, there exists such that

    Show that satisfies the desired property.

  2. The inclusion always holds as the preimage by a continuous function of an open set is open. To show the reverse inclusion, prove that every open ball in can be written as a countable union of balls . Now show that

    To show that the inclusion may fail for

  3. The implication always holds. Use the previous point to prove that weakly measurable functions in separable Banach spaces are measurable.

  4. By the Hahn Banach theorem, a linear subspace is dense if and only if it separates points. That is, for all there exists such that . Use this to show that the space spanned by is dense in .

The following characterizes the space of strongly measurable functions

Theorem 1 (Pettis measurability theorem) A function is -strongly measurable if and only if is -almost everywhere separately valued and is strongly measurable for all .

Proof. We first prove the implication. For some ,

As a result, takes almost everywhere values in the separable space spanned by the countably many . To see that is -strongly measurable we note that are simple functions and

We now prove the reverse implication. By assumption, there exists such that and is separable. As a result, and by point of Exercise 1 there exists a norming sequence .

Since we only need to prove limits almost everywhere, we can suppose that is separably valued by restricting to . Now, by assumption the functions are -strongly measurable. Let be the support of and . By definition of strongly -measurable function and since the union of countable sets is countable, is -finite. Since separate points, is outside of , and by restricting to we can suppose that is -finite.

Let be a countable dense subset of . Given , we define to be the which is the closest to , and where in the case of a tie we take the one with the smallest index . We can now define the simple functions

The function takes at most -different values with

Since is -strongly measurable, we deduce that the following function is measurable almost everywhere.

So, without loss of generality, we may suppose they are measurable by restricting once more. Then, by (2) is measurable. Since we had restricted to be -finite, we may take a partition of with finite measure. Consider , we have that is simple (we need to multiply by so that the support of the indicators have finite measure) and converges to almost everywhere.

If we include in the norming sequence of Theorem 1, we have that as in the proof above is bounded by almost everywhere and obtain the following corollary which will be used later to show the density of simple functions in the integrable ones.

Corollary 1 Let be -strongly measurable. Then, there exists a sequence of simple functions converging to almost everywhere and such that

Most typically, one works in the case where is -finite (for example, if is the Lebesgue measure or any probability measure) and identifies functions that are equal almost everywhere. In this case, the following more simple statement holds.

Theorem 2 (Pettis theorem for -finite measures) Let be a -finite measure space and identify functions that are equal almost everywhere. Then is -strongly measurable if and only if is separately valued and measurable.

Proof. Let be -strongly measurable. Then, is the limit of separately valued and measurable functions. As a result, is separately valued and measurable.

Suppose now that is separately valued and measurable. Then, the same is true for where is a partition of with finite measure. Moreover, these functions are strongly measurable as the measure of is finite (one can start from and form as in the previous proof converging to ) and since they form a partition of ,

By a basic argument, the limit of -strongly measurable functions is -strongly measurable and we conclude that is -strongly measurable, as desired.

4 Construction of the Bochner integral

Since the norm is a continuous function, given a measurable function , the real-valued function is also measurable. As a result, we can define the Lebesgue integrals

Likewise, the sets are in the -algebra and we can define

where the infimum is defined to be if the set is empty.

Knowledge of the real-valued case shows that (3) and (4) define a seminorm on the spaces of measurable function where they are finite, and they define a norm if we define the equivalence relation

Following common practice, we will identify functions that are equal almost everywhere from now on and make no distinction between functions and their equivalence classes. Finally, we arrive at the following definition.

Definition 3 Given a measure space , a Banach space we define the space of -integrable functions as (the equivalence classes)

If one wishes to be explicit about the underlying measure space one can also write . As in the real case, the spaces are complete.

Theorem 3 (Fischer-Riesz) The space is a Banach space for all

Proof. The proof follows along the lines of the real case, substituting the absolute value in by the norm in as necessary. We first prove the case Recall that a normed space is complete if and only if every absolutely convergent series converges.That is, we need to show that if is such that

Then there exists such that

To do so, one first applies <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minkowski_inequality#:~:text=.-,Minkowski

Thus, the sum is finite almost everywhere. Since is complete, we have that the above sum converges pointwise almost everywhere to some function

Furthermore we have that is strongly measurable as it is the limit of strongly measurable functions. Finally, using Fatou’s lemma for real-valued functions and the triangle inequality for norms shows that

Which shows convergence in for .

For the case consider a Cauchy sequence and write

By construction, and thus have measure zero and converges uniformly on . As a result, converges almost everywhere to some . This completes the proof.

Just as in the case of Lebesgue integrals, the proof of the completeness of serves to show that every convergent sequence must have a subsequence converging almost everywhere. This proposition is not necessary for the rest of the constructions, it’s just a nice property to have in reserve.

Proposition 1 Let , then there exists a subsequence converging to almost everywhere.

Proof. In the proof of the above proposition, we saw that for any absolutely convergent sum converges almost everywhere to its limit. Further, since is Cauchy, we can extract a subsequence with . By construction, the sequence

is normally convergent and converges in . By the above discussion we conclude the proof.

Ok, so we’ve constructed some spaces of -integrable functions and shown that they are complete. You know where this is going. Next stop is density town. In the standard construction of the Lebesgue integral, it is used that every measurable function to can be pointwise approximated by simple functions. One can achieve the same result for arbitrary metric spaces if the image of is separable.

Proposition 2 Every function in is the limit almost everywhere and in the norm of a sequence of simple functions.

Proof. By Corollary 1, there exists a sequence of simple functions converging to almost everywhere and such that almost everywhere. By the dominated convergence theorem for real valued functions, we have that

As a corollary, we obtain the following

Corollary 2 The simple functions are a dense subset of .

Since is complete, we have that the closure of with the norm is . Furthermore, by the triangle inequality (1), integration is continuous with this norm. This continuity allows us to extend integration to and shows that the space of integrable functions is .

Definition 4 We define the integral on as the unique continuous extension with the norm of the integral on . That is, given we define

Where is any sequence such that .

Remark 1. We could also work with the spaces

These spaces are once more complete, however, they do not contain simple functions as a dense subset. As a result, given it is not possible to make sense of the expression .

5 Familiar properties

Many properties of integration hold for the Bochner integral as well. For example, the following is a result of the definition of the Bochner integral and a passage to the limit, as it holds for simple functions.

Corollary 3 Let with a Banach space, then

  2. Let be another Banach space and . Then

Knowledge of scalar-valued results also goes a long way; for example, we can prove the following

Exercise 2 (Fubini-Tonelli) Let and be -finite measure spaces and consider the space with the -algebra generated by sets of the form with and the unique measure such that . Let be a Banach space and be strongly measurable. Then

  1. The functions and are strongly measurable.

  2. If any of the following integrals is finite

    Then all of the integrals in (5) are equal, and

TipHint

  1. From Fubini’s theorem, we know that the sections and are measurable. Since is separately valued so are and . As a result, by Theorem 2, they are strongly measurable.

  2. By <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fubini

    By Point of Corollary 3 and, since we just proved that all the integrals in (6) are well defined, we may pull out of the integrals. By the Hahn-Banach theorem, separates points of , and the proof follows.

Sometimes the following theorem is more useful when instead of using the product measure on , we use its completion . In this case, Fubini-Tonelli’s Exercise 2 still holds. It is only necessary to note that the sections of are now almost always measurable (see (Tao 2011) page 203 for more details).

Exercise 3 (Minkowski’s integral inequality) Show that given and it holds that

TipHint

Apply the triangle inequality (1) with .

Exercise 4 (Dominated convergence theorem) Let be such that almost everywhere and there exists such that almost everywhere. Then

TipHint

We have that almost everywhere. As a result, by the dominated convergence theorem for real-valued functions, we have that

The triangle inequality concludes the proof.

The dominated convergence theorem is a powerful tool that allows us to pass to the limit under the integral sign. For example, it can be used to show that, under necessary conditions, if is continuous (differentiable) in then so is (see here for a proof). These results, together with the density of simple functions, can be used to prove the standard approximation theorems.

Theorem 4 (Convolution, regularization and smooth approximation) Let and consider and .

  1. Young’s inequality. The convolution

    is well-defined almost everywhere and satisfies

  2. Mollifiers. Define . Then,

  3. Smoothing. If then with

  4. Smooth approximation. The space is dense in .

Ok, that’s it. This post was a bit more technical than some of the others, but you get the picture. Define an integral for simple functions, and figure out what can be approximated by simple functions. As we saw, the extra requirement that appears over the Lebesgue case is that the function is separately valued and justifies why, as we will see in future posts on SPDEs, the image of is often taken to be some separable Hilbert space. Until the next time!

6 References

Tao, Terence. 2011. An Introduction to Measure Theory. Vol. 126. American Mathematical Soc. https://www.stat.rice.edu/~dobelman/courses/texts/qualify/Measure.Theory.Tao.pdf.
Vakhania, Nicholas, Vazha Tarieladze, and S Chobanyan. 2012. Probability Distributions on Banach Spaces. Vol. 14. Springer Science & Business Media. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-94-009-3873-1.

item.title: MC 1: The chaos expansion
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
This is the first post of many to come on this blog, thanks for joining! This is also the first in a series on Malliavin calculus. This chain is based on the book (Nunno, Øksendal, and Proske 2008), and inspired by a reading group on the topic.

1 Wiener Itô chaos expansion

1.1 Three line summary

  • Square integrable deterministic functions of multiple variables can be repeatedly Itô integrated to get a square-integrable random function.

  • Repeated integration satisfies an isometry.

  • Every square-integrable function can be uniquely written as the sum of repeated integrals.

1.2 Why this is important

The chaos expansion gives us a way to represent random variables as a sum of functions in . This representation can be used to define the Skorokhod integral (Llamazares–Elias 2022).

1.3 Iterated integrals

We will write for the completion of the natural filtration generated by , on some measure space and consider an interval or . In this second case we say that We also recall the notation

We will work with the spaces of respectively square-integrable functions, symmetric square-integrable functions, and square-integrable functions on

Since these functions don’t involve a random variable we call them deterministic. Given we denote its symmetrization by

Where is summed over all the permutations of . Note that if is already symmetric then . Given a deterministic function we can transform it into a random one by taking its Itô integral. By integrating multiple times we obtain the following definition

Definition 1 Let , then we define the n-fold Itô integral as

The definition requires some care to make sense of and to make sure it is well defined. For example, note that the variables appearing in the upper bounds of the integration domain are the same as those appearing as integration variables. To better understand the definition, first note that, by the properties of the Itô integral, for each ,

Where is viewed as a function of . Due to the above inclusion is Itô integrable and

Where the above is viewed as a function of . Iterating this process we get that and

To notice the subtleties involved, we note that one example of an integral that would be ill-defined however is if for we defined

In this case, we have that the first integral is adapted and not adapted so we cannot continue integrating! As a result, we instead give the following definition for symmetric functions.

Definition 2 Let , then we define

Where it is important to note that the second equality is by definition and where the rescaling factor is motivated by the fact that, by counting permutations, if is finite then .

Proposition 1 (Itô’s n-th isometry) Let then

Proof. The proof is an application of Itô’s (product) isometry to move the expectation into the iterated integrals. If the number of integrals is unequal () you get the expectation of an Itô integral which is zero. Otherwise, you just get the (deterministic) inner product.

As a result, we also get by a calculation that if then

1.4 The chaos expansion

Lemma 1 (Itô’s representation theorem) Let , then there exists a unique such that

Furthermore it holds that .

Theorem 1 (Chaos expansion) Let , then there exists a unique sequence of functions such that

Proof. The proof is a bit technical but we sketch the main idea which is to iteratively apply Itô’s representation theorem to get Where and so on. By an iteration we deduce that if we write for the last integral term, then

Furthermore, by Itô’s -th isometry we have that the terms in the above sum are orthogonal so

Since the last term is bounded, we obtain a bound on the norm of the sum uniform in so the sum converges. As a result, so does to some . It suffices to see that . This can be proved using that, by Itô’s -th isometry, is orthogonal to for any and . As a result the limit is orthogonal to for all and by a density argument (there are a lot of these functions !) we obtain . This proves the first sum of the theorem.\\ It remains to prove the second part, but this follows by extending the by on and taking to be the symmetrization of this extension.

2 References

Llamazares–Elias, Liam. 2022. “The Skorokhod Integral.” https://nowheredifferentiable.com/2022-05-29-Malliavin-Calculus-2/.
Nunno, Giulia Di, Bernt Øksendal, and Frank Proske. 2008. Malliavin Calculus for lévy Processes with Applications to Finance. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-540-78572-9.

item.title: MC 6: Study of densities part 2
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • Solutions to SDEs of the form are Malliavin differentiable if .

  • Their Malliavin differential can be written as a stochastic integral.

  • This gives us an SDE linear in and can be solved exactly to obtain an explicit expression for .

2 Notation

The same as in the other posts of this series. In particular, we recall the notation for the set of progressively measurable square integrable stochastic processes. Furthermore, given a stochastic process such that for each we write for the Malliavin differential at time of . That is, if

is the chaos expansion of for each then we have that

3 Introduction

As anticipated in the summary, we will be working with an SDE of the form

It is a classical result of the theory of SDEs that, if and are Lipschitz continuous, then the above equation has a unique solution for each initial data . That is, there exists a unique continuous adapted process such that

Our goal will be to obtain an explicit expression for the derivative of . We will do so by directly differentiating in the expression above. As a result, we will need two lemmas that tell us how to differentiate each of the above integrals. The first of these is as follows.

Lemma 1 If is Malliavin differentiable for almost all . Then is Malliavin differentiable and we have that

Proof. Suppose that is progressively measurable. Then, using the previously studied divergence property and the fact that the Skorokhod integral is an extension of the Itô integral gives

We consider the chaos expansion of . Then, as was seen previously, we have that

So, writing the chaos expansion for gives

Substituting in the first equation we derived shows that

As a result, we only need to show that is progressively measurable for all . This follows by some knowledge of how the Malliavin differential works with conditional expectations. We haven’t covered this so we refer the reader to (Nunno, Øksendal, and Proske 2008) page 34.

Our second lemma shows how to differentiate deterministic integrals. In this case, we need a stronger condition than existing for each fixed .

Lemma 2 Let be Malliavin differentiable for each with

Then, given it holds that

Proof. We will apply Fubini, we have that

Where both Fubini and the commutation of the sum and the integrals are justified by the condition of the lemma, which guarantees that the last sum has finite norm as

Where in the first inequality we applied Fubini, Cauchy Schwartz, and the triangle inequality, and in the equality, we used our old calculation of the norm of the Malliavin derivative

The result now follows by noting that, by the linearity of the iterated integrals, it holds that the terms

Is the chaos expansion of .

In particular, by setting , this shows that

That is, we can commute the derivative with deterministic integrals. The previous two lemmas together with the chain rule show that, if we take , and the solution to our SDE verifies all necessary conditions, then

Proposition 1 Given our SDE (3) with it holds that there exists a unique solution and for all we have

Proof. The proof is quite technical and we merely sketch it. The full detail in (Nunno, Øksendal, and Proske 2008) page 120. By the previous discussion, it is only necessary to show that verifies the conditions of the lemma, i.e. is Malliavin differentiable and its differential verifies that

This is proved by a Picard iteration

The aim is to prove that are differentiable with

For the case this is clear as we have that

For the general case, the condition of the Lemma 1 is a consequence of the hypothesis of induction on and the chain rule. Verifying the conditions of Lemma 2 (and in fact stronger bounds on the supremum of ) can be done using the Burkholder–David–Gundy inequality. Once that is done, one can prove through a discrete version of Gronwall’s inequality that are bounded uniformly in . Since we know by classical theory of SDEs that this is sufficient to show that

Completing the proof.

We now show how to obtain an explicit expression for by using that the equation verified by is linear (in as opposed to ). Doing so uses a generalized version of Ito’s formula for stochastic coefficients.

Theorem 1 Let and verify the SDE

Then is Malliavin differentiable on with

Proof. Let us fix any and set. Then

Then we have that, since are bounded, and for each fixed it holds that

Where we define . Symbolically we have the family of linear SDEs starting at time

Consider

Which solves the differential equation

Applying Itô to gives

Setting proves the result by the uniqueness of solutions as both sides verify the same SDE (note that has the same stochastic differential as but now takes initial data ).

We end this post by noting that Proposition 1 has a multidimensional generalization which can also be found in Nualart’s book (Nualart and Nualart 2018), on page 119.

4 References

Nualart, David, and Eulalia Nualart. 2018. Introduction to Malliavin Calculus. Vol. 9. Cambridge University Press. https://books.google.co.uk/books?hl=zh-CN&lr=&id=l_1uDwAAQBAJ&oi=fnd&pg=PR11&dq=nualart+introduction+malliavin&ots=_JuMhMkTMt&sig=Tx5y00u4kMNs73jLtMEs-kyXAuU&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q=nualart\%20introduction\%20malliavin&f=false.
Nunno, Giulia Di, Bernt Øksendal, and Frank Proske. 2008. Malliavin Calculus for lévy Processes with Applications to Finance. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-540-78572-9.

item.title: MC 7: The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Semigroup
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • There is a natural extension of the Laplacian to the Wiener space.

  • The Laplacian generates the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup.

  • The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup in finite dimensions is generated by the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, from which it derives its name.

2 The Laplacian of a random variable

First, we give some finite-dimensional motivation. Suppose that and . Then an integration by parts shows that the adjoint of the gradient in is minus the divergence. That is,

Then, we define the Laplacian as minus the adjoint of the gradient composed with the gradient

Which gives the familiar .

Of course, this is all well and good when the domain of is a finite-dimensional space. Otherwise, there is no Lebesgue measure. We now move to what is our base case in our series of blog posts and consider a probability space where is generated by a Wiener process . Then, as we have seen previously, Skorokhod integral is the adjoint of the Malliavin derivative so we would like to define

On what kind of random variables can we define this? Well let us take with a rapidly decaying chaos expansion, then

All we require for this expression to make sense is that- the right-hand side is in . That is, by Ito’s -th isometry, that

Is this a space we’ve dealt with before? Well if we recall our old spaces [}. Then we have that Where analogous calculations go through if we have more derivatives to get the terms . This shows that

Thus, the domain of is exactly . This is quite pleasing as, as we have observed earlier, the spaces mimic the Sobolev spaces , when this resemblance is quite strong as we have that the norm on is

Which is formally equal to the one just derived for It is very interesting to observe that, directly from the definition, we obtain a basis of eigenvalues of . Let us define

That is, are the random variables that only have the -th term in their chaos expansion to be non-zero. Then by the chaos expansion theorem, we know that

And by construction of the Laplacian, for every . In fact, by the uniqueness of the chaos expansion, the elements of for some are the unique eigenvectors of

3 The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup

As it turns out, defines a semigroup

Definition 1 The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup is the family of operators

The term is quite reminiscent of the semigroup for the heat equation

and will cause an analogous smoothing effect by making the terms in the chaos expansion to decrease faster. To see that defines a semigroup first note that, by the linearity of the iterated integrals,

So as a result

Which shows that . Finally, note that

Where the commutation under the integral sign (with the counting measure) is justified as is uniformly bounded in . There’s an explicit formula for .

Proposition 1 (Mehler’s formula) Let be the Wiener space, then

The proof is technical and can be found in Nualart’s book (Nualart and Nualart 2018) on page 74. Let us try to understand the formula and also the reason for the name of the semigroup. We consider as at the beginning of this post the finite-dimensional case but now with some Gaussian measure

Then, integration by parts shows that

That is, the adjoint of the gradient in is . Notice that we get the extra term that corresponds to multiplication by .As a result, the Laplacian on is given by

Furthermore, by Itô’s formula, is the generator of the SDE

Let us write for the solution to the above SDE with initial data That is, if we define then

The process that solves the SDE above is known as the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process and, by the theory of linear SDEs, is given by

Since

We deduce that for each fixed we can find with

We then get that

And by taking we recover Mehler’s formula. This correspondence is expanded on in chapter of Hairer’s notes (Hairer 2009).

4 References

Hairer, Martin. 2009. “An Introduction to Stochastic PDEs.” arXiv Preprint arXiv:0907.4178.
Nualart, David, and Eulalia Nualart. 2018. Introduction to Malliavin Calculus. Vol. 9. Cambridge University Press. https://books.google.co.uk/books?hl=zh-CN&lr=&id=l_1uDwAAQBAJ&oi=fnd&pg=PR11&dq=nualart+introduction+malliavin&ots=_JuMhMkTMt&sig=Tx5y00u4kMNs73jLtMEs-kyXAuU&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q=nualart\%20introduction\%20malliavin&f=false.

item.title: Martingales in Banach spaces
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • Conditional expectations exist in a natural way for simple functions, by taking extensions they also exist for integrable functions to a Banach space .

  • Using conditional expectations we can define what a martingale is just like in the real case.

  • The space of continuous -integrable martingales is a Banach space.

2 Why should I care?

Banach valued martingales form the basis of SPDEs. This is because analogously to Itô integration of real-valued processes. Integrating against a Wiener process valued in a Banach space the same will produce a square integrable continuous martingale.

3 Conditional expectation

In graduate-level probability courses, given a -algebra one shows that by applying Radon–Nikodym’s theorem, for any real-valued random variable there exists a conditional expectation verifying that

Of course, now that we have created an integral for integral random variables to a Banach space we would like to see whether such a conditional expectation also exists for these functions. If we are given a simple function

It is a simple calculation to show that, since are real-valued and thus are well defined, then

verifies the desired formula. Furthermore, we have that is a linear, and pointwise continuous operator with

This allows us to show the following:

Theorem 1 (Existence and uniqueness of conditional expectation) Let for some Banach space . Then has a conditional expectation satisfying

Proof. We have already proved the above inequality for simple processes. By our previous post on the Bochner integral, we can take converging to in to obtain that

As a result, is a Cauchy sequence in and converges to some function , passing to the limit in the defining equation for the conditional expectation shows that . Finally, to prove uniqueness we have that if both satisfy

Then using the linearity of the integral we obtain that for all linear functions , so .

4 Martingales

Okay, so we leveraged some inequalities to prove the existence of a conditional expectation. This done, the following definition mimicking the real case is quite natural. Given measurable spaces and , we use the notation to mean is measurable.

Definition 1 Let be a stochastic process on with a filtration . The process is called an -martingale, if:

  1. for all ,

  2. for all ,

  3. for all .

The concept of submartingale is defined by replacing the equality in 3. with a . Let us abbreviate by . Then, as in the real case, we have the following.

Lemma 1 (Norm: martingale submartingale) Let be a martingale, then is a submartingale.

Proof. We recall that, by the Hahn Banach theorem, it holds for any metric space that given

As a result, by the linearity of the integral and abbreviating the supremum to just ,

Let us recall the following result for real-valued martingales (Çinlar 2011, Theorem 3.2.6).

Lemma 2 (Doob’s maximal Martingale inequality) Let be a real-valued submartingale. Then it holds that

As a consequence, if is left (or right) continuous then

The idea of the above result is that, since is a submartingale, . Getting from the continuous to the discrete case is possible by using the continuity of and approximating it on some finer and finer mesh . This said, applying Doob’s maximal martingale inequality together with Lemma 1 gives that

Theorem 2 (Maximal Inequality) Let and let be a separable Banach space. If , is a right-continuous -valued -martingale, then

Proof. This follows by using that is a submartingale and Doob’s maximal inequality.

Doob’s inequality is essentially an equality between different function norms we can place on the space of continuous Martingales and will provide a very powerful tool later on.

Corollary 1 Let be a (left or right) continuous martingale to a separable Banach space . Then the following are equivalent

Where we recall from the previous post that symbolizes that may not be separately valued and only have an integrable norm. That said, the same reasoning shows that the above result also holds for the integrable spaces.

A useful space of Martingales is as follows

Definition 2 Let be a valued martingale with index set , then we define

and give it the norm

By Theorem 2 we have that

and that any of the norms of these spaces is equivalent to the one set on . This is useful in the following result

Proposition 1 Let be a separable Banach space, then is a Banach space.

Proof. By the previous observation and the completeness of the spaces proved in the previous post, is a subspace of a Hilbert space. As a result, it is sufficient to show that it is closed. Let converge to . Then, by the equivalence of the norms we have that so that for all

This shows that is a martingale. Furthermore, as was seen in the previous post, there exists a subsequence such that

Since are continuous and continuity is preserved by uniform limits this proves that is continuous almost everywhere. This concludes the proof.

In future instalments, we will prove that a Wiener process valued in a Hilbert space belongs to this space and use it to define the stochastic integral that leads to the construction of SPDEs.

Proposition 2 Let be a separable Hilbert space and be a symmetric, non-negative, trace-class operator on . Let be an -valued -Wiener process with respect to a filtration . Then .

Proof. The process is adapted by definition. Let . Given and , the mapping is a bounded linear functional. Since is independent of , we apply the properties of the Bochner integral to obtain:

This satisfies the definition of conditional expectation, proving , so is a martingale. Finally, by construction as is trace-class. Continuity is given by the definition of the Wiener process. This concludes the proof.

5 References

Çinlar, Erhan. 2011. Probability and Stochastics. Springer.

item.title: The Itô integral
item.dc:creator: Liam Llamazares--Elias
item.description:
1 Three line summary

  • The Itô integral is a way of integrating random variables against Brownian motion.

  • The Itô integral is well defined for piecewise constant adapted processes and turns them isometrically into square integrable continuous martingales ().

  • As a result the Itô integral can be extended isometrically to a function . Furthermore can be characterised explicitly as the square integrable adapted processes that are measurable in time and space.

2 Why should I care?

The Itô integral forms the basis of the whole of stochastic calculus. This comprises SDEs, SPDEs. Knowledge of what functions can be integrated and what properties the integrated function has is instrumental. In this post we construct the integral and address both of the preceding issues.

3 Notation

Given two measure spaces we abbreviate that is measurable between and as Furthermore we will take or to be the index set of our stochastic processes and by abuse of notation write to mean in the former case and in the latter.

4 Progressive measurability

Perhaps the best way to understand a -algebra is as a set of available information. With this interpretation we understand as the information available to us by time . A basic property of a stochastic process is that at time they are determined by this information.

Definition 1 A stochastic process valued in a measurable space is adapted if for all .

As we will soon see the only stochastic process that can be integrated are the square integrable and progressively measurable. But what does this mysterious term mean?

Definition 2 A stochastic process valued in a measurable space is progressively measurable if its restriction to is measurable as a map for all .

Whenever we’re given a stochastic process and a filtration the first thing to check is that it is adapted. In fact, since is measurable for all we have that the following holds.

Lemma 1 Progressively measurable processes are adapted.

Additionally, stochastic processes can be viewed path-wise but also be seen as functions of a product space, this leads to the following definition.

Definition 3 We say that a stochastic process valued in a measurable space is jointly measurable if where .

In the definition of progressive measurability we imposed some kind of measurability, in fact the condition leads to the following

Proposition 1 (Progressive implies jointly measurable) Let , and be progressively measurable. Then it is also jointly measurable.

Proof. Let be a sequence converging to and with if . Given we have that

Note however that the converse isn’t true, for example if is constant in then, for some

So it suffices to consider some Construction where but . It is also important to note the following.

Lemma 2 (SDE coefficients are progressive) Let be a progressively measurable stochastic process and let be measurable, then is progressively measurable.

Proof. This follows from writing the map as the composition of and . The former map evaluates to a measurable function on with respect to , since is measurable and is measurable by the progressive measurability of .

The difference between progressively measurable and adapted is quite subtle. In fact every adapted and jointly measurable stochastic process has a progressively measurable modification (see (Karatzas 1987) page ). The proof of this fact is very lengthy and technical. Thus, if (and in particular is jointly measurable) is adapted, we may always choose a representative that is progressively measurable. This leads to some authors giving the definition of the class of Itô integrable functions in terms of joint measurability instead of progressive measurability. In the end both lead to equivalent definition. That said, this technicality is usually of little importance due to the following result.

Lemma 3 (Continuity is progressive) Let be a left or right continuous stochastic process. Then is progressively measurable.

Proof. Fix . We will show that the restriction of to is measurable. Suppose for example that is right continuous, then we consider the approximations

and . The pre-image of any set under is of the form

Since is adapted, , meaning the above set is in . We conclude as by right continuity for all . The pointwise limit of measurable functions is measurable. Also we used that, for the same reason, the pointwise limit of progressively measurable functions is progressively measurable.

5 Construction of the integral

As for Riemann integration our construction relies on step functions, here called elementary processes.

Definition 4 We say that is an elementary process if there exist increasing and such that

Lemma 4 For any , the space of elementary processes is -dense in the space of progressively measurable processes. That is, for any there exists a sequence of elementary processes such that

A detailed proof is given in . This density immediately yields the closure of elementary processes.

Theorem 1 Let be the space of elementary stochastic processes adapted to , equipped with the norm. Its completion is exactly

The proof of this is by the previous approximation result together with the fact that the Ito integral of elementary processes is an isometry and the fact that is complete. This last property follows from the completeness of the spaces and the fact that pointwise limits of progressively measurable functions are progressively measurable (and from every convergent sequence in we can extract a convergent sub-sequence which must also converge to the limit). This finally leads us to be able to define the stochastic integral.

Theorem 2 Let and define for a elementary process Then the above defines an isometry to the space of continuous square integrable martingales as

Thus, it extends uniquely to the closure . Furthermore the extension also has image in

Proof. The first part of the proof is a calculation using the definition of integral of elementary process, the adaptedness of and the definition of . The second part is slightly more tricky. The fact that is a martingale is due to convergence ( would suffice). Then, one takes a sequence of elementary processes converging to . By the first part one may apply Doob’s martingale inequality and convergence to get a measure of the set where the supremum

which can be made small for . One can then extract a subsequence and apply Borel-Cantelli to deduce that the above supremum goes to almost everywhere. This shows that is almost everywhere Cauchy in and thus converges almost everywhere to some continuous process . This process must be by convergence to which concludes the proof.

6 References

Karatzas, Ioannis. 1987. “Brownian Motion and Stochastic Calculus.” Elearn. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-1-4612-0949-2.
